Ekhymosis is a rare medical condition involving the inflammation of the external ear, most often due to the invasion of the ear canal by foreign objects. It is sometimes associated with the use of ear jewelry, such as earrings, and the incorrect placement of ear piercings. It can also be caused by the use of headphones, earmuffs, and hearing aids. Ekhymosis is most common in young children, as they tend to insert foreign objects into their ears. It is also more common in men than in women. The most common symptoms of ekhymosis are ear pain, tenderness, and swelling. In some cases, the ear may also bleed, and a discharge of pus may occur. Diagnosis is based on the history of the condition and an ear examination. In some cases, a sample of the discharge may be sent for analysis. Treatment involves the removal of the foreign object from the ear. This may be done by a healthcare professional or by the patient themselves. After the object is removed, the ear should be cleaned with a solution of hydrogen peroxide and water. To prevent recurrence, it is important to avoid inserting foreign objects into the ears. Ear jewelry should be properly placed and cared for, and headphones and earmuffs should be used appropriately. Ekhymosis is a rare condition involving the inflammation of the external ear. Eduardo Kac (born November 10, 1962) is a Portuguese-American artist and professor of art at the University of Wisconsin–Milwaukee, where he is the director of the Center for Deep Listening. His work includes performance art, computer music, video, installation art, sculpture, artificial life, painting, and poetry, and is focused on the relationship between art, technology, and the human body. Kac was born in Portugal in 1962, and raised in Portugal and the United States. He studied art at the University of Illinois at Chicago, the School of the Art Institute of Chicago (BFA, 1984), and Stanford University (MFA, 1987). Kac's work often addresses the themes of life and death, and the relationship between the body and technology. He has created a number of performance art pieces in which he uses his own body as a canvas, incorporating technology such as robotics, genetics, and virtual reality. Kac's work has been exhibited internationally, and is in the collections of the Museum of Modern Art, the Guggenheim Museum, and the San Francisco Museum of Modern Art, among others. Kac has been the recipient of numerous awards and fellowships, including a John Simon Guggenheim Memorial Fellowship (2003), a National Endowment for the Arts Fellowship (1994), and a New York Foundation for the Arts Fellowship (1997). Kac is the author of several books, including The Body and the Network (2014), and he has been a contributor to numerous publications and journals. Kac's work has been the subject of a number of retrospective exhibitions, including "Eduardo Kac: A Light in Dark Times" at the San Francisco Museum of Modern Art (2018), and "Eduardo Kac: bioartography" at the Museu blow-up doll in Praga, Prague (2018). Kac lives and works in Milwaukee, Wisconsin.

The 2018–19 Liga MX Femenil season is the second season of the Liga MX Femenil. The tournament is organized by the Mexican Football Federation and is divided into two tournaments: the Apertura 2018 and the Clausura 2019, each divided into two phases. In the first phase of each tournament, the teams were divided into eight groups of four teams, in which all teams played each other once, and the first two teams in each group advanced to the next phase. The next phase of the tournament consisted of a quadrangular group for each tournament, in which the winners advanced to the final. The Apertura 2018 tournament began on 7 July 2018 and ended on 19 October 2018. The Clausura 2019 tournament will begin on 5 January 2019 and end on 2 April 2019. América was the defending champion, having defeated UNAM Pumas in the previous tournament's final. Guadalajara was the first team to qualify for the Apertura 2018 final, after winning its fourth game in a row to finish with 28 points. It was the first time a team had qualified for the final without losing a single game. The team ultimately won the tournament after defeating Toluca in the final. América was the first team to qualify for the Clausura 2019 final, after defeating Toluca in the first leg of the tournament final. It was the second time in two tournaments that the team had reached the final. The team ultimately won the tournament after defeating Toluca in the final, winning its second consecutive title.

Kantana Group is a production company established in Bangkok, Thailand in 1951 by Pradit and Somsook Kaljaruek. Kantana took its first step from radio dramas to films, television dramas, variety programs, and eventually moved on to become an integrated entertainment company.

History
Kantana began in 1951 as a radio drama troupe founded by Pradit and Somsook Kaljaruek. The company branched out into television in 1958 with the drama series Ying Kor Mee Hua Jai.

The company has since expanded to include the production of feature films, commercials and documentaries.

Structure
The business operations of the company and its subsidiaries are divided into three core areas: television production, film and post production, and "edutainment" and government relations.

Television

Family and variety programs
Family and variety shows are produced for Thai television channel 5 (Royal Thai Army TV), BBTV Channel 7 and TITV. The shows include the cartoon series, The Adventure of Khan Kluay.

Drama series
Around 18 drama series, or lakorns are produced for BBTV Channel 7 and TITV.

Film

Kantana Animation Studio
Kantana Animation produced the first Thai computer-animated feature film in 2006, Khan Kluay, which took three years and cost 150 million baht to make. Khan Kluay was the top film at the Thailand box office in 2006, earning 91 million baht, and winning several awards. Also, a Khan Kluay television series was created for BBTV Channel 7. Kantana Animation was spun off into its own subsidiary in 2007, Kantana Animation Studio Company, Limited.

Post-production
Kantana is a hub for film post-production services in Asia. Kantana's film lab provides overlay subtitling, negative cutting, color analysis, digital intermediate processing, optical effects, sound transfer, film cleaning and film print copying. The facility has provided lab work on such films as Memoirs of a Geisha, Superman Returns, Casino Royale, Echo Planet and dozens of Thai feature films.

The company's sound studio provides audio dubbing and mixing services for feature films, television shows and commercials, including recording, sound effects, foley, sound design and voice dubbing/ADR. Kantana was the first Thai company to receive permission from Dolby Laboratories to record sound using Dolby technology.

Oriental Post
Oriental Post Company, Ltd., which is 50-50 joint venture by Kantana and Loxley Video Post, provides digital post production facilities. The services include digital compositing in PAL, NTSC, HD 24p in multi-format, telecine, digital editing and special effects creation. Using digital intermediate technology, Oriental Post's services also include film processing, scanning conforming, color correction, offline and online editing, digital effects, computer graphics and audio recording to film printing. The company has provided post-production work on the films of Wong Kar-wai, 2046 and My Blueberry Nights.

The Castillo de San José (Castle of San José) is an historic fortress and art museum in Arrecife on the Canary Island of Lanzarote.

History
Built between 1776 and 1779 the fort was constructed to provide a defensive stronghold in case of pirate attacks, and as a public works project to provide much needed employment during a time of famine and poverty on the island. It became commonly known as the Fortress of Hunger (Fortaleza del hambre). The famine was caused by a number of factors including periods of drought, and the earlier eruption of Timanfaya between 1730 and 1736, which devastated most of the productive agricultural areas on the island. Charles III of Spain, who was concerned for the plight of the islanders, ordered that the fortress be built.
 
Situated on a cliff above the Port of Naos, the D shaped fort has semi-circular walls facing the sea. On the landward side, the rear wall is protected by two small turrets, with a moat crossed by a lifting drawbridge leading to the main gateway. The fortress is constructed of masonry and ashlar blocks of volcanic origin. The interior which is made up of barrel vaulted rooms was mainly used as a powder store.

International Museum of Contemporary Art
In the 1970s the fortress was renovated and the interior redesigned by the artist César Manrique, who was born nearby, to house a museum of modern art.  The museum opened to the public in 1976 as the International Museum of Contemporary Art (, abbreviated as MIAC).

The exhibits are primarily from the period between 1950 and 1980, with a focus on various abstract art forms, such as modern sculpture, kinetic art, and geometric abstraction. The works are mainly by Spanish artists including Joan Miró,  Antoni Tàpies, Eusebio Sempere and the El Paso group, one section is also dedicated to the Canarian artist Pancho Lasso.

The courtyard is also used as a display space for outdoor sculptures and designs.
Beneath the museum, the annexe of the fort was renovated and converted into a restaurant, the two  connected by a spiral staircase. With curved floor to ceiling windows, the dining room has panoramic views overlooking the harbour and docks below.

This is a list of Hong Kong Twenty20 International cricketers. A Twenty20 International is an international cricket match between two representative teams, each having Twenty20 International status, as determined by the International Cricket Council (ICC). A Twenty20 International is played under the rules of Twenty20 cricket as laid down by the ICC, except for those modifications detailed in the ICC's Twenty20 International regulations. Hong Kong has played Twenty20 International matches since 2006, when it hosted the Hong Kong Twenty20 International Cricket Festival at the Hong Kong Cricket Club. At the 2007 ICC World Twenty20, Hong Kong played its first Twenty20 International match against Bangladesh, though the match did not have Twenty20 International status. Hong Kong's first Twenty20 International match with such status was against Pakistan in the 2008 ACC Trophy, and it has played Twenty20 International matches annually since. The Hong Kong national cricket team has played in every Asia Cup since the 2002–03 edition, and has played in every ICC World Twenty20 since the 2007 edition. Hong Kong also played in the 2010 ICC World Twenty20 in the West Indies, and in the 2012 ICC World Twenty20 in Sri Lanka. As of 20 September 2012, Hong Kong has played 26 Twenty20 International matches, resulting in 7 victories, 7 defeats, and 12 no results. This list is of players who have played at Twenty20 International level. It is initially sorted by the players' last names, but can be sorted on any of the columns, except the "caps" column, which is only used for the purpose of allowing a player to be sorted in the order in which he made his debut. To find a player in the list, a name can be typed into the "search" box. To sort the list in a different order, first ensure that the "sort by" box is set to the column to be used to sort the list. Then, to change the order in which the column is sorted, either click on the column heading or use the arrow buttons. The list can also be searched for a player by typing his name into the "search" box and selecting "Go". However, this will only display players who have played at Twenty20 International level, and will not display players who have only played in other matches that have been granted Twenty20 International status. Players in this list must have played in at least 1 Twenty20 International match, and is initially sorted in the order in which the players made their debut.

Project TL (The Lineage) is an upcoming massively multiplayer online role-playing game (MMORPG) by NCSoft. It is part of the Lineage series and a sequel to the first Lineage game.  The game was first announced and titled as Lineage Eternal in November 2011 but has suffered numerous delays in its release schedule, with the earliest beta testing planned. The first South Korea Closed Beta began in 2016, starting on November 30 and ending on December 4. NCSoft has rebooted the game as Project TL in NCSOFT Director Cut private event.

Development
The game has suffered from numerous delays during development. NCSOFT officially announced Lineage Eternal as the sequel to the first Lineage, released in 1998 in November 8, 2011. The first gameplay videos debuted at the G-Star 2011 gaming convention in South Korea on November 9. In August 2013, NCSOFT was preparing to roll out the beta schedule of Lineage Eternal by the end of that year. The developers planned to initiate beta testing in Korea towards the end of year of 2015, but during a conference call in November they confirmed that closed beta testing would be delayed until '16.

In NCSOFT Q1 2017 earning conferences, NCSOFT had said that the development team of Lineage Eternal had significant changes due to the change of game producer. The new team continued the development process of the game with a new target and changes to the game engine from a proprietary game engine that was used in Guild Wars to Unreal Engine 4. The game has changed to Project TL in 2017.

Alexandra (Alix) Evonne Hui (born 1980) is an American historian of science specializing in the history and psychophysics of sound, and especially of sound studies in 19th- and 20th-century Germany. She is an associate professor of history at Mississippi State University, and co-editor-in-chief of Isis.

Education and career
Hui is originally from California, and majored in physics at Pomona College, specializing in astrophysics and astronomy and graduating in 2001. She went to the University of California, Los Angeles for graduate study in history, earning a master's degree in 2003 and completing her Ph.D. in 2008.

She joined the  Mississippi State University history department as an assistant professor in 2008, and was promoted to associate professor in 2014. She has traveled to Norway as a Fulbright Scholar in 2014–2015, and to Germany as an Alexander von Humboldt Fellow in 2017–2019.

As well as serving as co-editor for the History of Science Society and its journal Isis, Hui is a member of the council of the American Historical Association.

Books
Hui is the author of the monograph The Psychophysical Ear: Musical Experiments, Experimental Sounds, 1840–1910, published in 2013 by the MIT Press in their book series Transformations: Studies in the History of Science and Technology. She is also a co-editor of two edited volumes on sound, Music, Sound, and the Laboratory from 1750-1980 (a special issue of Osiris in 2013) and Testing Hearing: The Making of Modern Aurality (Oxford University Press, 2020).

Castillo de San José The Castillo de San José, located in the city of Casablanca, is one of the most important artillery pieces in the history of Spain. It is the oldest still in existence and the second largest in the world. It was built between 1764 and 1774, during the reign of King Charles III, to protect the city of Portsmouth from attacks by the British. It was designed by the Italian engineer Giampaolo Temi, who was also responsible for the design of the Fort of San Christóbal in San Fernando, Cádiz. It is a star fort with an unusual shape, in the form of a crown. The walls are 1,5 meters thick and the total perimeter is 1,200 meters. The fort has a central courtyard, with a covered area of 1,600 square meters, and has a chapel and a barracks. After the visit to the fort, the guide will take us to the Hall of the Tapers, where the original ones are still preserved. Next, we will go to the Weapons Hall, where we will find a great variety of weapons used in the defense of the fort. We will finish the visit at the top of the fort, where we will enjoy a wonderful view of the mouth of the port of Casablanca, the beach, and the Bay of Algeciras.

The National Museum of Contemporary Art, Athens (EMST) is a museum in Athens, Greece, dedicated to contemporary art. The museum is located in the former Fix brewery, which was built in the early 20th century. The brewery was in operation until 1984, when it was closed after a long period of decline in the beer industry in Greece. In the early 2000s, the building was adapted for use as a museum by the architectural firm Meletakm, which preserved many of the original industrial features of the building, such as the old machinery and pipes. The museum opened in 2007, and since then it has hosted a number of exhibitions of contemporary art. The museum's collection includes works by many prominent artists, including Alexander Calder, Christo, Paul Klee, and Andy Warhol. The museum also has a library and archive, and it organizes a variety of events and educational programs. In recent years, the museum has been the subject of some controversy. In 2013, it was reported that the museum's director, Nicholas Bouras, had been accused of mismanaging the museum's finances, and that the museum was in debt to the tune of several million euros. In 2015, the museum was closed for a year due to a funding crisis, and it only reopened in 2016 after receiving a bailout from the Greek government.

MN1 (gene) The MN1 gene (mobile element derived 1 gene) is a gene that codes for the protein Meis1. The gene is located on the short arm of chromosome 20 (20p11.2). It spans 3.3 kb and comprises 3 exons and 2 introns. The MN1 gene encodes a protein called Meis1, a DNA-binding protein that is a transcription factor and regulator of cell differentiation and growth control. The gene is expressed in all tissues, with higher levels in testis and lower levels in kidney. The MN1 gene is evolutionarily conserved, with homologs in both vertebrates and invertebrates. In humans, the gene has 4 alternatively spliced variants, 3 of which encode the same protein and 1 that is a pseudogene. The MN1 gene is involved in the regulation of cell growth and differentiation. It is a transcription factor that binds to the DNA and regulates the expression of target genes. The gene is involved in the regulation of cell cycle, cell growth, and cell differentiation. The MN1 gene is associated with the development of several cancers, including acute myeloid leukemia, chronic myeloid leukemia, and acute lymphocytic leukemia. The gene is also associated with the development of Wilms' tumor, a type of kidney cancer. The MN1 gene is a tumor suppressor gene, which means that it helps to regulate cell growth and differentiation and can prevent cells from becoming cancerous. The gene is thought to be involved in the regulation of cell cycle, cell growth, and cell differentiation. New London Harbor Light is a lighthouse in Connecticut on the west side of the New London harbor entrance. It is the nation's fifth oldest light station and the seventh oldest U.S. lighthouse. It is both the oldest and the tallest lighthouse in Connecticut and on Long Island Sound, with its tower reaching 90 feet.

The light is visible for 15 miles and consists of three seconds of white light every six seconds. It was listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 1990. It is currently owned and maintained by the New London Maritime Society as part of the National Historic Lighthouse Preservation Act program.

Location
New London Harbor Light is located at Lighthouse Point in southern New London, just east of Guthrie Beach off Pequot Avenue.  In addition to the lighthouse, the station includes the keeper's house, a two-and-a-half-story brick residence.  The property once also included a barn, an oil house, and an engine room; all of these structures have been removed.

History

The original New London Harbor Lighthouse was built on the west side of the entrance to New London Harbor in 1760. Connecticut ceded the lighthouse to the United States according to the "Memoranda of Cessions" of 7 August 1789.

On May 7, 1800, Congress appropriated funds to rebuild the lighthouse, and it was removed in 1801 when the current stone tower was built. In 1855, a fourth-order Fresnel lens replaced the original 11 lamps with  reflectors.  Illumination was converted to oil-vapor lamp in 1909 and to acetylene in 1912.  The light was electrified in 1930.  The present keeper's house was built in 1863, and was enlarged in 1900 to accommodate the families of married keepers.  The light was acquired by the New London Maritime Society in 2010.

Aditya Kapadia (9 August 1968 – 5 November 2020) was an Indian actor, director, producer, and screenwriter. He was the founder and creative head of the production house 'Midday Movies'. He was best known for directing the award-winning film, 'Nishikant'. In 2015, he won the Best Director Award at the Indian Film Festival of Houston. Kapadia began his career as an actor, with his debut in the 1984 film, 'Aaj Ke Naam Doon'. In 1986, he appeared in the television serial, 'Khandaan'. He then appeared in the 1987 film, 'Pyar Ke Kabil'. In 1988, he appeared in the hit film, 'Qayamat Se Qayamat Tak', where he played the role of a supporting actor for the first time. He then appeared in the 1989 film, 'Dost Garibon Ka'. In 1991, Kapadia made his directorial debut with the film, 'Maa', which was an adaptation of the hit Malayalam film, 'Mammootty'. The film was a hit, and Kapadia was nominated for the Best Director Award at the Indian Film Festival. In 1994, he directed the film, 'Jaan Ki Kasam', which was an adaptation of the Hollywood film, 'Obedience'. The film was a hit, and Kapadia was nominated for the Best Director Award at the Indian Film Festival. In 1996, Kapadia directed the film, 'Dushman Duniya Ka'. The film was a hit, and Kapadia was nominated for the Best Director Award at the Indian Film Festival. In 1998, he directed the film, 'Iski Sadi'. The film was a hit, and Kapadia was nominated for the Best Director Award at the Indian Film Festival. In 2000, Kapadia directed the film, 'Nishikant', which was an adaptation of the Hollywood film, 'The Jacket'. The film was a hit, and Kapadia was nominated for the Best Director Award at the Indian Film Festival. In 2002, he directed the film, 'Dil Hai Tumhaara'. The film was a hit, and Kapadia was nominated for the Best Director Award at the Indian Film Festival. In 2008, Kapadia directed the film, 'Hijack'. The film was a hit, and Kapadia was nominated for the Best Director Award at the Indian Film Festival. In 2012, he directed the film, 'Shobhna aur Sanjiv'. The film was a hit, and Kapadia was nominated for the Best Director Award at the Indian Film Festival. Kapadia died on 5 November 2020 due to a heart attack.

Syria–Turkey relations Syria and Turkey have a very complex relationship, which has been marked by both cooperation and conflict. During the early 20th century, the two countries were part of the Ottoman Empire. Syria gained independence in 1946, and Turkey did the following year. Since then, the two countries have been in a state of rivalry and competition, although they have also been allies at various times. The two countries have a number of common cultural and historical ties. Both are majority Muslim countries, and the cultural and historical ties between the Syrian and Turkish people go back to the days of the Ottoman Empire. The two countries also share a border, which has been the source of some tension in recent years. In the modern era, Syria and Turkey have been in a state of competition and rivalry. This has been most evident in the region of Cyprus, where the two countries have been in a state of conflict since the 1950s. In addition, the two countries have also been in a state of competition in the region of Syria itself, where Turkey has been trying to assert its influence. In recent years, the two countries have been in a state of cooperation as well. In 1998, they signed an agreement to cooperate in the area of security, and in 2002 they signed an agreement to cooperate in the area of economy. In addition, the two countries have also been working together to try to resolve the situation in Cyprus.

Jeremy Hutchinson, Baron Hutchinson of Lullington (30 May 1898 – 8 June 1979) was a British Conservative politician, lawyer, soldier, and businessman. Hutchinson was the son of Sir William Edward David Hutchinson and his wife, Kate (née neville). He was educated at Eton College, an all-boys public school in Berkshire, England. He served in the British Army during the First World War, achieving the rank of captain in the Royal Sussex Regiment. He then attended Trinity College, Cambridge, where he gained a Bachelor of Arts in Law in 1921. Hutchinson was called to the bar in 1923 at the Middle Temple, one of the Inns of Court. He worked as a lawyer and in 1924 joined the staff of the Conservative Party's research department, for which he wrote the party's local government policy. In 1927, he was made a member of the Royal Commission on the Press. In 1929, he was elected to the House of Commons for Liverpool Waterloo, a constituency in the city of Liverpool, England. He served as a Conservative Member of Parliament (MP) for Liverpool Waterloo until he lost his seat in the general election of 1945. In 1939, he was elected to the House of Lords, where he sat as a Conservative peer. Hutchinson was a member of the National Council of the National Trust and in 1956 he became the chairman of the National Trust's properties in London and the Home Counties. He was also a member of the council of the Shakespeare Memorial Theatre at Stratford-upon-Avon, a member of the Royal Fine Arts Commission from 1949 to 1954, and a member of the Royal Commission on the Press from 1947 to 1949. Hutchinson was the author of The Press and the Public (1930), a book about the relationship between the British press and the public. Hutchinson married Lady Leonora Mary, daughter of William George Ward, 2nd Earl of Dudley, in 1930. They had three children, including the journalist and author Jeremy Hutchinson.

Tang Man Sit v Capacious Investments Ltd [1999] 4 SLR 257; 1999 (2) SLJ 305 This case concerned a company (the "Company") which was listed on the Stock Exchange of Hong Kong. The first and second respondents (the "Majority Shareholders") held approximately 76% of the issued shares of the Company. The other respondent (the "Minority Shareholder") held approximately 24% of the issued shares of the Company. The issue before the Court of First Instance (the "CFI") was whether the Minority Shareholder was entitled to vote at a general meeting of the Company. At a previous general meeting, the Majority Shareholders had purported to amend the articles of association of the Company (the "Articles") to restrict the right of the Minority Shareholder to vote at general meetings. The Minority Shareholder applied to the CFI for an interim injunction to restrain the Majority Shareholders from enforcing the amendment to the Articles. The CFI held that the amendment to the Articles was invalid and that the Minority Shareholder was entitled to vote at the general meeting. The Majority Shareholders were granted leave to appeal to the Court of Appeal (the "CA"). The issue before the CA was whether the Majority Shareholders had amended the Articles in accordance with the requirements of the Companies (Winding-Up and Miscellaneous Provisions) Ordinance (the " Ordinance"). The CA held that the Majority Shareholders had not complied with the requirements of the Ordinance and that the amendment to the Articles was invalid. The Majority Shareholders were granted leave to appeal to the Court of Final Appeal (the "CFA"). The issue before the CFA was whether the Majority Shareholders had amended the Articles in accordance with the requirements of the Ordinance. The CFA held that the Majority Shareholders had not complied with the requirements of the Ordinance and that the amendment to the Articles was invalid.

Located Clarksville in Howard County, Maryland, United States, Huntington Farms.

Huntington Farms is a two-story L-shaped brick house started in 1840 by John T. Hardey. Hardey's brother Dr. William H. Hardey, built Montrose next door in 1844. The house was built during the antebellum slavery period in the Howard District of Anne Arundel County, which later became Howard County. In 1875, the house was destroyed in a fire and rebuilt in its current configuration. The property contains remnants of a brick kiln that was used by slaves to fire bricks, and a graveyard that contains family and slaves. The graveyard containing Nicholas Hardy, buried in 1850 is now located between two pipe-stem driveways in the subdivision.

The surrounding farmland has been reduced from 185 acres to 13 acres in 1977 and subdivided to only 3.085 acres around the original house for residential development.

Political positions of Benigno Aquino III Benigno Aquino III was born to a well-known political family in the Philippines. His father, Benigno Aquino, Jr., was a highly respected opposition leader who was assassinated in 1983. His mother, Corazon Aquino, was a former First Lady who succeeded his father in the opposition after his death and was later elected as the first female president of the Philippines. Throughout his life, Aquino III was never involved in politics, instead choosing to pursue a career in the private sector. He worked as a financial analyst in various investment banks and companies in the United States and Hong Kong. He also served as an adviser to his mother during her presidency. Aquino III's political beliefs were aligned with his mother's democratic principles, and he was known for being a strong advocate for good governance, transparency, and reform. He was also a vocal critic of corruption and the abuse of power, and he worked to improve the lives of the poor and underprivileged. Aquino III was elected to the Senate of the Philippines in 2007, and he served as a senator until his resignation in 2012 to run for the presidency. He was sworn in as the 15th president of the Philippines on June 30, 2010, and he served until June 30, 2016. As president, Aquino III implemented a number of reforms aimed at improving the economy, fighting corruption, and strengthening the democratic institutions of the Philippines. He also worked to promote international relations and diplomacy, and he was widely respected and admired by the international community. Aquino III was the first Philippine president to be born after the country's independence from the United States. He was also the first to be born in a non-royal family, the first to be born a Catholic, and the first to be born in the province of Tarlac. Aquino III was married to Tarlac City Mayor Maria Leonor "Leni" Robredo. They had two children, Maria Aurora "Misa" and Benigno "Nino". Aquino III died on June 24, 2021, at the age of 65 from complications related to diabetes.

Darcy Pattison (born 1957) is an Australian writer, illustrator, and designer of children's books. In the 1980s, Pattison established an art studio in Melbourne, and her first commission was for the Melbourne Museum, to illustrate a book about the platypus. Her work has since been exhibited at the National Gallery of Australia, the National Gallery of Victoria, and the Queensland Art Gallery. Pattison has written and illustrated more than 30 children's books, including Possum Magic, which has sold more than one million copies. Her books have been published in 15 languages, and have won many awards, including the Children's Book Council of Australia (CBCA) Picture Book of the Year, which she has won three times. Her work has also been included in the International Board on Books for Young People (IBBY) honour list. In 2012, Pattison won the CBCA Eve Pownall Award for her book The Cloud. In 2013, her work was included in an exhibition at the National Gallery of Australia, titled Australian Picture Book Illustration: The Greats from the Collection.

The Dominion Timber and Minerals Railway was a private standard-gauge railroad that operated in the Laurentian Mountains of Southwestern Quebec, Canada from 1916 to 1981. Owned by Canadian Refractories Limited, the railway's 20km mainline stretched from CRL's magnesite mine in Kilmar, through an extremely rugged mountain route to its final terminus at Marelan. Originally a narrow-gauge railway from 1916 to 1933, the Dominion Timber and Minerals Railway expanded to include a 65-ton locomotive and several freight cars.

Due to rising operating costs and decreasing sustainability, the railway was decommissioned in 1981, with many of the railway ties being dumped into lakes along the route by workmen. Until 2012 the abandoned rail route has been popular among ATV and dirt bike riders, however the upcoming sale of the land will permanently limit trespassing on this now-closed trail.

Castle Mountain is a remote 8,620-foot (2,630-meter) mountain summit located at the southeastern edge of the Wrangell Mountains, in the U.S. state of Alaska. The peak is situated  northeast of McCarthy at Skolai Pass in Wrangell-St. Elias National Park and Preserve. Although modest in elevation, relief is significant as the south face rises 4,000 feet above the terminus of the Russell Glacier in less than one mile. Precipitation runoff from the peak drains east via the White River, and west to the Nizina River via Skolai Creek. The mountain's descriptive name was reported in 1914 by the United States Geological Survey.

Climate

Based on the Köppen climate classification, Castle Mountain is located in a subarctic climate zone with long, cold, snowy winters, and cool summers. Weather systems coming off the Gulf of Alaska are forced upwards by the Wrangell Mountains (orographic lift), causing heavy precipitation in the form of rainfall and snowfall. Temperatures can drop below −20 °F with wind chill factors below −30 °F. The months May through June offer the most favorable weather for viewing and climbing.

The Living Christ Series is a 1951 12-part drama television series about the life of Jesus Christ directed by John T. Coyle.

Plot
A television mini-series told the life of Christ in a version of the typical Hollywood serial film. Each episode was thirty minutes long, bringing the total running time of the series to what might have been considered an epic six hours long if the films had been shown consecutively. Filmed in color in 1951, it is notable for being one of the few American film series of that time in which Christ's face was actually shown. Since the early days of sound, American film producers had been afraid that some members of the audience would be alienated if an actor who did not fit the public's image of Christ was chosen to play the role, so virtually all had played it safe by showing Him only from the back or in long shot. Sometimes only Christ's hand would be seen. Foreign film makers, however, had no such doubts, and Christ was actually shown in such films as the French Golgotha (1935), the Mexican El Martir del Calvario (1952), and the 1953 British serial Jesus of Nazareth.

However, The Living Christ Series was not meant to be shown in movie theatres, but more as an instructional, inspirational film in churches or on Sunday-morning television. Far from being a religious epic, the series was clearly shot mostly on soundstages and on a low budget, much like a typical television program of the era shot on film. Directed by John T. Coyle, the series featured mostly unknown actors or actors who appeared mostly in B-films. A notable exception was Lowell Gilmore, who played Pontius Pilate, and whose most famous previous role was as Basil Hallward in MGM's classic 1945 film The Picture of Dorian Gray. Character actor Lawrence Dobkin also appeared in the series, and Will Wright, perhaps most famous for appearing as Ben Weaver on several episodes of The Andy Griffith Show, was cast as Herod. Martin Balsam also plays a small role. Screen unknown Robert Wilson, who would go on to practically make a career out of playing the role, portrayed Christ. Noted movie trailer announcer Art Gilmore served as narrator.

The Living Christ Series, like other similar religiously-themed films, was released by Cathedral Films. It has been released on VHS and DVD, both in separate volumes and in a boxed set.

Gregory Haerr (born September 8, 1948) is an American murderer and suspected serial killer, currently serving three life sentences for the 1978 murder of three women in Kentucky. Haerr was born in 1948 in Michigan. He had a difficult childhood; he had a strained relationship with his stepfather, and his shyness made him a frequent target of bullying. Later, however, his intelligence and social skills enabled him to enjoy a successful college career, and he developed a series of apparently normal emotional relationships with women. In 1978, Haerr was working in his first professional job, as a computer programmer at the Fort Knox Federal Credit Union, when he was arrested and charged with the murder of three women in Kentucky. He had allegedly approached the victims in bars and other public places, and convinced them to accompany him to isolated areas, where he would then strangle them. In one case, he allegedly told the victim that he was capable of killing her, but that he would not do so if she agreed to have sex with him. After the murders, Haerr would take the victims' money and identification, and sometimes other personal items, to make it appear as if the women had disappeared voluntarily. At the time of his arrest, Haerr was in a relationship with a woman named Martha, who was aware of his history of attracting women and taking them to isolated areas, but was unaware of his violent tendencies. Martha later said that Haerr had once forced her to have sex with him in an isolated area, but that she had escaped unharmed. After a brief and unsuccessful courtship, Haerr married Martha in 1979, while he was awaiting trial. Two months later, he strangled her to death in a hotel room, in what he would later claim was a suicide. He then called police and reported that he had found her body. In 1980, Haerr was convicted of the three murders, as well as the murder of his wife. He received three life sentences, as well as an additional 60 years in prison. He has not been charged with any other murders, but police believe that he may have killed as many as 20 women. In 2008, Haerr was among a group of prisoners who filed a class action lawsuit against the Kentucky Department of Corrections, claiming that they were denied proper medical treatment. The lawsuit was settled in 2015.

Cancio Garcia (born 1973) is a Spanish actor, model, and businessman. In 1996, Garcia was chosen by the Hollywood producer James Bond to be the new James Bond, after the death of the actor Sean Connery. The decision was not easy, as the producers had to choose between several actors who were candidates to replace Connery, including Colin Salmon, who would eventually play the role of Charles Robinson, and the actor and producer Roger Moore, who would play James Bond in four films. In 1997, Garcia starred in the film Tomorrow Never Dies as James Bond, with the actress Michelle Yeoh as his partner, Wing chun teacher and expert in martial arts. The film was a great success, becoming the highest-grossing film in the history of the series, breaking the record set by Goldeneye (1995). In 2007, after the success of Casino Royale (2006), Garcia's agent said that he was ready to return to the cinema with a new James Bond adventure, entitled Quantum of Solace. In the end, the role was played by the actor Daniel Craig.

The 1935–36 New York Rangers season was the tenth season for the team in the National Hockey League (NHL). The Rangers finished the season in last place in the NHL, and did not qualify for the playoffs. The New York Rangers played in their tenth season in the NHL, and their sixth in the Madison Square Garden. The Rangers finished the season in last place for the second consecutive year, and did not qualify for the playoffs. The team collected a record of 11–29–12, and finished with 44 points. The Rangers had a 0.300 points percentage, which was their worst since the 1928–29 season, when they finished with a 0.268 points percentage. The team's total attendance was 662,507, their lowest since the 1927–28 season. The Rangers' average home attendance was 9,391, their lowest since their first season in the Garden. The Rangers' general manager, Lester Patrick, decided to rebuild the team through youth, and traded away many of the team's older players. The team's average age was 26.6 years, which was the youngest in the league. The average age of the Rangers' players was younger than the league average by 2.2 years. The Rangers' head coach, Frank Boucher, left the team to become the head coach of the New York Americans. The Americans had joined the NHL in the previous season, and played in the Garden. The Rangers' former coach, Lester Patrick, returned to the team, and coached the team for the remainder of the season.

Veena Music Veena music is the name given to music that is played on the veena. The veena is a generic term for a plucked instrument, with many variations, and is thus somewhat similar to the guitar. Although the veena may be found in many different regions, it is typically associated with the Indian subcontinent. The oldest veena in India is the veena dussels, which dates back to the 3rd century BC. It is a very large instrument, with a neck that can be as long as 5 feet (1.5 m) and is made out of a single piece of wood. The body of the instrument is made out of a large piece of log, and the instrument is played by sitting on the ground. The veena dussels was traditionally used as a solo instrument, and as such, is typically associated with the Indian classical music tradition. However, it is also used in many other genres of music, including folk, pop, and filmi. The most common veena in India is the veena ekaki, which is a smaller instrument, with a neck that is typically around 2 feet (0.6 m) in length. It is played by holding the neck in one hand, and plucking the strings with the fingers of the other hand. The veena ekaki is used in both solo and ensemble performances, and is a key instrument in many different genres of music, including Indian classical music, folk, pop, and filmi. It is also used in many different regional styles, such as the Carnatic music of South India, and the Hindustani music of North India. The veena gauvau is another type of veena that is popular in India. It is similar to the veena ekaki, but has a deeper body, and is played by sitting on the ground. The veena gauvau is used in both solo and ensemble performances, and is a key instrument in many different genres of music, including Indian classical music, folk, pop, and filmi. It is also used in many different regional styles, such as the Carnatic music of South India, and the Hindustani music of North India.

Alexandra Hui is a Canadian politician who was elected to in the Yukon Legislative Assembly in the 2011 election. She was sworn in as a member of the Yukon Legislative Assembly on November 7, 2011, and was appointed Minister of Justice and Minister of Labour in the cabinet of Minister of Justice and Minister of Labour in the cabinet of Yukon Premier Darrell Pasloski. Hui was born and raised in Toronto, Ontario. She attended the University of Toronto, where she earned a bachelor's degree in political science, and the London School of Economics, where she earned a master's degree in international relations. Hui moved to Yukon in 2007 to work as a federal civil servant in the Department of Indian and Northern Affairs. In 2009, she was hired as executive assistant to the minister of justice in the Yukon government. Hui was elected to the Yukon Legislative Assembly in the 2011 election, defeating incumbent MLA Kevin Barr by five votes. She was the first Chinese Canadian and the first woman of Chinese descent to be elected to a legislature in Canada. Hui was appointed Minister of Justice and Minister of Labour in the cabinet of Yukon Premier Darrell Pasloski on November 7, 2011. She was the first woman to hold the position of justice minister in Yukon. In 2012, Hui introduced a bill to amend the Yukon Legal Services Act to allow non-lawyers to own and operate a law firm. The bill was passed by the Yukon Legislative Assembly in 2013. Hui was re-elected in the 2011 election, defeating incumbent Yukon Party candidate and former minister Richard Mostyn by 15 votes. Hui was sworn in as a member of the Yukon Legislative Assembly on November 5, 2009, and was appointed Minister of Justice and Minister of Labour in the cabinet of Yukon Premier Darrell Pasloski. She was the first woman to hold the position of justice minister in Yukon. Hui was re-elected in the 2011 election, defeating incumbent Yukon Party candidate and former minister Richard Mostyn by 15 votes. Hui was elected to the Yukon Legislative Assembly in the 2011 election, defeating incumbent MLA Kevin Barr by five votes. She was the first Chinese Canadian and the first woman of Chinese descent to be elected to a legislature in Canada.

Charles Kleiber (30 October 1887 – 13 November 1954) was a German conductor, particularly associated with the works of Richard Wagner. Kleiber was born in Leipzig, where he studied musicology under Arnold Schering and Paul Leuckert. In 1912 he took up conducting, and in 1916 became assistant to the conductor of the Royal Court Opera in Berlin, where he assisted Richard Strauss in the world premiere of his opera Daphne. He then became conductor at the Magdeburg Opera (1919–21), and at the Beethovenhalle in Bonn (1921–23), where he conducted the first performance of the complete Ring cycle (on four consecutive nights, 10–13 October 1923) since the premiere in 1876. In 1923, Kleiber married the actress and singer Elfriede Lorang; they had one daughter, Susanne, who became a singer. They were divorced in 1937. In 1924, Kleiber became conductor at the Opera in Dresde, where he remained until 1938. He also conducted regularly at the Berlin State Opera and the Munich State Opera. He was appointed musical director of the Berlin State Opera in 1928, but declined the offer, since he did not wish to be associated with the Nazi regime. In 1938, Kleiber became a professor at the Berlin College of Music. In 1941 he was appointed General Music Director in Damascus, Syria. He remained there until 1949, when he was appointed conductor of the Bavarian State Opera in Munich. He was also an occasional conductor at the Bayreuth Festival, making his debut there in 1951 with a production of Parsifal, and conducting the Ring cycle in 1953. Kleiber died in Munich in 1954, aged 67.

Shah Jahani Mahal The Shah Jahani Mahal is an architectural structure in the city of Shahjahanabad, in the Indian capital of Delhi. The palace, which dates back to the 17th century, was built by the Mughal emperor Shah Jahan to serve as the residence of the imperial family. The Shah Jahani Mahal is located in the heart of Delhi, near the Red Fort and the Chandni Chowk market. The palace is built in the traditional Mughal style, with a large central courtyard and a series of surrounding chambers. The courtyard is surrounded by a series of arcades, with the chambers located behind these. The chambers are all connected to the courtyard, and are accessed through a series of gateways and passageways. The building is constructed using red sandstone, with white marble used for the gateways and the interior of the chambers. The Shah Jahani Mahal served as the residence of the imperial family, and was also used for formal occasions and ceremonies. The building is a major example of Mughal architecture, and is a major tourist attraction in Delhi.

Adrian Favell is Chair in Sociology and Social Theory at the University of Leeds and chercheur associé of the Centre for European Studies at Sciences Po, Paris. He is also a Professorial Academic Associate of the Sainsbury Institute for the Study of Japanese Arts and Cultures and serves as an associate editor of Journal of Ethnic and Migration Studies and on the editorial committee of Journal of Common Market Studies.

Academic career
Adrian Favell has been Professor of Sociology at Sciences Po. Before that he was the Director of Centre for Regional and Global Ethnographies and Professor of European and International Studies at Aarhus University and Professor of Sociology at UCLA.

His research on migration studies has contributed to debates on citizenship, multiculturalism and integration, intra-EU migration, and high skilled migration. He argues for the necessity of sophisticated comparative approaches, which recognise the asymmetry between nation-state contexts of immigration, particularly in comparisons between Britain and France, or European nations and the US and Canada. His work on intra-EU "Eurostars", which uses ethnography as a method, is widely noted as having pioneered study on the everyday consequences of European integration on younger generations of highly mobile European citizens.

His book, Before and After Superflat, is the first academic history in English of contemporary art in Japan since 1990. In Japan, the book became notorious after the artist Yoshitomo Nara angrily challenged online some of its assertions about his mode of organisation and business practices  and.

Unicase (also known as unicase, MixCase, mixed case, or casefolding) is the ability of some letter systems to write all letters, or certain letters, with either a lower or upper case letter. In most cases this refers to the ability to use either the upper or the lower case of the same letter. A unicase script is a writing system with a unicase base alphabet. Most writing systems are unicase, with notable exceptions including the Han script, Devanāgarī, and most abugidas. The basic Latin alphabet is unicase, as is the Greek alphabet. Cyrillic and the basic Hebrew alphabet are also unicase. Most letters in the unicase scripts are displayed in upper case in all caps settings, and lower case in lower case settings. However, some letters in unicase scripts are not Capitalized in all caps settings, such as the soft-gamma in Cyrillic, Ẽ in the Latin alphabet, and Ạ in the Greek alphabet. In some unicase scripts, notably the Arabic and Hebrew scripts, some letters (or all letters) have distinct shapes for upper and lower case, but are never joined with a superior diacritic to create a single letter. The Arabic and Hebrew scripts are referred to as "semicased" rather than unicase in this context. The term "unicase" was coined in 1969 by Peter R. Jones to describe the behavior of letters in the Latin and Greek alphabets. The term is sometimes confused with "lowercase", "upper case", or "case sensitive". A unicase alphabet is an alphabet that includes at most one letter for each sound, and whose letters are normally written in only one case. A unicase letter is a letter that can be represented by at most one character in a given alphabet. A unicase script is a script that uses only unicase letters, or that can be represented entirely by unicase letters from some alphabet. A unicase writing system is a writing system that uses only unicase letters, or that can be represented entirely by unicase letters from some alphabet. A unicase font is a font that includes only unicase letters. A unicase encoding is an encoding that maps letters to the same byte value in both the upper and lower case. A unicase keyboard is a keyboard that produces only unicase letters. A unicase character is a character that has the same form for both upper and lower case. A unicase word is a word that is formed from unicase letters only. A unicase text is a text that contains only unicase words.

William "Plum" Smith (sometimes erroneously spelt Smyth) (26 January 1954 – 8 June 2016) was a Northern Irish loyalist, former paramilitary, and politician. He had been involved in Ulster loyalism in various capacities for at least forty years.

Early life
Smith was born in Mountjoy Street on Belfast's Shankill Road into a poor Ulster Protestant family, the son of shipyard worker Charles William Smith and his wife Isobel. He had three older sisters, Margaret, Elizabeth and Nan (the latter dying in infancy before he was born), a younger brother Gordon and a younger sister Jean. There was rumoured Native American ancestry in his family; therefore in his youth he acquired the lifelong nickname "Plum" after The Beano character Little Plum. He was raised in a working class home where his parents sent him to Sunday school and taught him to respect the law. Like many of his contemporaries from similar backgrounds on both sides of the divide, the outbreak of the socio-political\religious conflict that came to be known as the Troubles in 1969 saw him become involved in paramilitarism.

Move to paramilitarism
Following the introduction of internment in 1971, Smith worked for a time as an orderly in Crumlin Road Gaol where he served six months for rioting against the British Army in the Highfield estate. Unbeknownst to the prison authorities, Smith was working as a mole for the imprisoned Ulster Volunteer Force (UVF) leader Gusty Spence, passing on information about the conditions in which the internees were being held.

Around this time Smith was a member of the vigilante group and was part of a group within the SDA that later became the Red Hand Commando, including founder John McKeague, who decided to form a new, more active organisation. In 1972, Smith was a founder-member of this new group, which quickly became an elite squad augmenting the UVF.

On 1 July Smith was one of two armed RHC men to meet Spence when he was released from prison to attend the wedding of his daughter to Winkie Rea. He took Spence to a meeting of the UVF leadership where a plan was hatched to keep Spence out of prison. A few days later when Spence was being returned to jail by his nephew Frankie Curry, their car was stopped on Belfast's Springmartin Road and Spence "kidnapped" by UVF/RHC operatives.

Soon after this incident Smith was himself arrested for his part in the attempted murder of Catholic civilian Joseph Hall, a drive-by shooting that Smith would later admit was motivated by "pure sectarianism and bigotry". Smith was handed a ten-year prison sentence for the shooting. Tried along with Ronnie McCullough and Tom Reid, Smith pleaded not guilty although he subsequently admitted that he did so to satisfy the UVF's policy of members never pleading guilty and would state that "we were caught red-handed".

In the Maze
Along with the likes of Billy Hutchinson, David Ervine and Billy Mitchell, Smith was one of those on the UVF wings of the Maze prison in the 1970s to be won over by Gusty Spence to his newer, more politicised, way of thinking. This cadre of Spence-trained political figures would go on to play a leading role in bringing about the UVF ceasefire in 1994 as members of the Progressive Unionist Party (PUP). He learnt Irish during his time in prison, and was happy to refer to the language as 'his own'. Smith was released on 28 July 1977 after serving five years of a ten-year sentence.

Political activity

Smith secured a job in the Harland & Wolff shipyard in 1978 and became involved in trade union activity, initially as a shop steward before ultimately ending up a member of the general executive of the Transport & General Workers Union. He was dismissed from the shipyard in 1988 following a period of restructuring that involved a sell-off of part of the business, although Smith contended that his trade union activity had hastened his removal by making him unpopular with the management.

At this point the unemployed Smith became involved with a number of community schemes on the Shankill Road that were organised by Gusty Spence, initially in a voluntary capacity. The two became close and when Spence undertook a reorganisation of the PUP in 1990, Smith was given the role of chairman and Prisons Spokesman, along with Hugh Smyth as leader, David Ervine as Political Spokesman and Spence and Jim McDonald as members of the party executive.

Through Spence, Smith established contacts with Joe Colgan, a Dublin-based Irish republican, and in March 1993 the two arranged an event in the city at which both a member of the UVF and the trade unionist Chris Hudson were in attendance. As a result of the meeting Hudson opened a regular channel of contact with the UVF through which he exhorted them to seek peace.

The Combined Loyalist Military Command (CLMC) ceasefire was announced on 13 October 1994 at Fernhill House, Glencairn when Gusty Spence read out a joint statement of ceasefire flanked by Smith and Jim McDonald of the PUP and Ulster Democratic Party representatives Gary McMichael, Davy Adams and John White. Smith was the chairman of the press conference at which Spence read out the statement. For a time he had served as Chairman of the Progressive Unionist Party itself.

Smith would go on to devote his attentions to community work with the Ex-Prisoners Interpretative Centre although he has remained a spokesman for the PUP. He was also an unsuccessful candidate for the PUP in the Belfast City Council elections in 2001 in the Court district electoral area.

Speaking out in 2012 about Unionist concerns at the Maze prison becoming an IRA shrine with a proposed Conflict Transformation Centre, Smith stated that if Loyalists themselves got involved in the scheme they could tell their side of the story and no one particular group could use it as a shrine.

Opposition to trials
In 2009 Smith was strongly critical of the report issued by the Consultative Group on the Past, as chaired by Robin Eames and Denis Bradley. He criticised the work of the Historical Enquiries Team, which investigated unsolved incidents from the Troubles, arguing that it was opening "a can of worms" and preventing "closure". He compared any attempts to reopen investigations and bring about criminal proceedings to the Nuremberg trials.

In 2010 Smith attacked the work of Victims Commissioner Brendan McAllister, who was investigating the activities of the UVF unit based on the Mount Vernon estate, north Belfast, during the Troubles. Smith argued that such investigations contravened a guarantee he had been given by Mo Mowlam that offences committed before 1998 could not be prosecuted due to a general amnesty. Soon afterwards Smith claimed that a document released by the British and Irish governments proved that the deal was in place as he claimed. In support of his stance Smith even gave evidence at the trial of Gerry McGeough, arguing that the republican should not have been tried for the 1981 attempted murder of Sammy Brush because of the supposed deal being in place.

Book
Smith launched his book Inside Man, Loyalists of Long Kesh – The Untold Story () on 13 October 2014, the twentieth anniversary of the CLMC ceasefire. The book argues that thanks to politicisation and education a group of prominent loyalists prisoners came to support negotiation with their republican counterparts, leading to the eventual Northern Ireland peace process. The book mainly dealt with the experiences of loyalist prisoners within the Maze and was not intended as an autobiography.

Death
Smith died on 8 June 2016, aged 62 after a short illness. Former PUP leader Brian Ervine paid tribute to his negotiating skills by bringing the RHC & UVF to the peace process, his intelligence, left thinking politics and for his cross community work. Sinn Féin Deputy First Minister Martin McGuinness praised his contribution and commitment to the peace process.

His funeral was held in St. Matthew's Church of Ireland on the Woodvale Road. Community worker and former loyalist prisoner Jim Wilson described Smith's contribution to loyalism as 'massive' during The Troubles but later became an 'architect of peace'. Wilson also commented that Sinn Féin representative's presence at the funeral proved Smith's sincerity at extending the hand of friendship across the sectarian divide.

Frank Dimant (born September 14, 1938) is a Canadian-American mathematician and computer scientist, known for his contributions to the field of combinatorics, in particular, for introducing the concept of a tropical polynomial. Born in Toronto, Ontario, Dimant received a Bachelor of Science degree in 1960 from the University of Toronto and a Master of Science degree in 1961 from the University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign. In 1964, he was awarded a Ph.D. in mathematics by the University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign under the supervision of John H. Conway. After receiving his Ph.D., Dimant took a position at the Mathematics Department of the University of California, Los Angeles. In 1968, he joined the IBM Research Division in San Jose, California, where he worked on the design and analysis of computer algorithms, and became an IBM Fellow in 1989. After his retirement from IBM in 2008, he joined the Mathematics Department of the University of California, Irvine, as a Distinguished Professor. In 1998, Dimant was elected a fellow of the Association for Computing Machinery "for contributions to the design and analysis of algorithms and the theory of computation." In 2000, he was elected a fellow of the American Academy of Arts and Sciences. In 2002, he was elected a fellow of the Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers "for contributions to the design and analysis of algorithms and the theory of computation." In 2009, he was elected a fellow of the American Mathematical Society. In 2013, Dimant was awarded the Knill–Tits Award by the University of Toronto for his contributions to the field of combinatorics. In 2015, Dimant was awarded the E. J. Hinchley Award by the University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign.

Hollis Gillespie (born June 15, 1961) is an American country music artist. A native of LaFayette, Georgia, she grew up on a farm, where she enjoyed riding horses. In her youth, she participated in rodeos and played both the guitar and the piano. Gillespie's first recording contract was with the independent label A&S Records, which released her debut single "I've Never Been to the Moon" in 1986. The song failed to chart, and she recorded a total of three singles for A&S before the label went bankrupt. In 1991, Gillespie signed with BNA Records, breaking through with the top ten hits "She's a Woman" and "Lenny and the Pissant Boys", the latter of which she co-wrote. Her debut album, Count On Me, was released that year, and while it received positive reviews, it failed to chart. Her second album, Home, was released in 1993. It also failed to chart, and she was dropped from BNA's roster. In 1997, Gillespie signed with the independent label River North Records, releasing the album Nobody's Angel that year. The album was produced by James Stroud, and its lead single "She's a Woman" peaked at No. 69 on the Hot Country Songs charts. Also in 1997, she recorded a cover of the song "I'll Be There for You", which was featured on the soundtrack to the film The Longest Kiss. She also covered "The Christmas Song (Chestnuts Roasting on an Open Fire)" for the compilation album Country Christmas Classics. In 2000, Gillespie recorded an album for the independent label Cornbread Records. Titled From My Heart, it was her first album to chart, peaking at No. 72 on the Billboard Top Country Albums charts. The title track was released as a single that year, and reached No. 73 on the Hot Country Songs charts. Also in 2000, she appeared on the song "This Is God" on country singer Larry Gatlin's albumLarry Gatlin & the Flight-Gatlin Band. In 2001, Gillespie appeared on the song "A Hard Man to Forget" on country singer Steve Wariner's album Faith in You. She released a single in 2006, "I Can't Sleep", which failed to chart. In 2013, she released a single "Strong as Steel" via her Facebook page.

Philippine President Benigno Aquino III has made political position on many national issues.

Energy policy

Nuclear energy

Aquino is not keen on utilizing nuclear energy as a remedy to a power shortage Mindanao experienced in the second quarter of 2012. Aquino said that while he is open to adopt nuclear energy he is more inclined to tap "other sources of energy that have less impact – or potential negative impact – that are available to us. While Aquino remained open to nuclear energy he has publicly expressed his opposition to plans to revive the moth-balled Bataan Nuclear Power Plant.

Environmental policy
During an interview in CNN in November 2013, Aquino said that countries "contributing immensely to the global warning" have a moral responsibility to contribute to end climate change.

Financial policy

Priority Development Assistance Fund
Following the Priority Development Assistance Fund scam, Aquino initially insisted that the abolition of the Priority Development Assistance Fund or PDAF is unnecessary. Aquino later detracted from his earlier statement and vowed to abolish the fund and replace it with a new system.

Social policy

Abortion and reproductive health
Despite his support for the Reproductive Health Bill, which was perceived by critics as a precedent to a law on legalizing abortion, Aquino insists that he is against the legalization of abortion.

Aquino supported the passing of the Reproductive Health Bill. He believes on the responsibility of the state to inform couples on their right to plan their family.

Foreign land ownership
Aquino is against revising the constitution to allow full land ownership by foreigners. Aquino remains firm in keeping the 60/40 foreign ownership law which guarantees Filipinos majority share on land ownership.

Gay marriage
Aquino has yet to make a firm stance on gay marriage. During a forum at the Sofitel Philippine Plaza in December 2013, Aquino was inquired on his view on gay marriage and if he was in favor of its legalization. Aquino initially refused to answer the question but later clarified his views on the issue. He said that while he believes on universal human rights he is undecided on the legalization of gay marriage. Aquino expressed some concerns that legalization of gay marriage may follow legalization of abortion. He said that the issue must first be viewed on a "child's perspective".He expressed concerns that children adopted by gay marriage couples may experience gender confusion.

Security policy

Gun ownership
Aquino, a gun enthusiast himself, is against the imposition of a total gun ban in the country. He believes in the right to bear arms. On May 29, 2013, Aquino signed the Republic Act No. 10591, an "Act Providing for a Comprehensive Law on Firearms and Ammunition and Providing Penalties for Violations thereof", providing a new set of standards on obtaining a license to bear firearms.

Height restriction on security personnel
Aquino vetoed a bill that would have remove height restrictions on police, fire and jail personnel. Aquino found the bill unnecessary as a waiver can be given to applicants under certain conditions. Aquino insists that jail personnel in particular "must possess the necessary physical attributes to perform their functions effectively".

Foreign policy

China
Aquino has made statements critical to China indirectly and directly. At the 2013 ASEAN summit in Brunei, He accused China of being uncooperative after it rejected to participate in a case filed by the Philippines to the United Nations tribunal. Aqunio also said that he is open of an idea of a joint development in the South China Sea but remained cautious and insisted that a joint development would be under Philippine law.

Aquino supports a diplomatic solution to the South China Sea dispute. He reiterated that ASEAN members should draft the Code of Conduct of Parties in the West Philippine Sea before commencing ASEAN-China talks.

Japan
Aquino supported a proposal to allow Japanese troops access to Philippine military bases along with another proposal to give American troops greater access to Philippine military installation. Aquino views these proposals as an avenue to build a credible alliance with Japan and the United States.

Candies Creek Ridge, also known (more commonly in the past) as Clingan Ridge, is a geographic feature ridge located primarily in Bradley County, Tennessee, on the southeastern border of the state. It overlooks Candies Creek and is in north Cleveland, Tennessee. 

Found here in 1996 was a large South Appalachian Mississippian culture village, built about 1000-1200 CE. It is unusual in this location, because Mississippian villages were typically found in the bottomlands along rivers and creeks. The 5-acre site was acquired for preservation by The Archeological Conservancy in 2001 and is known as the Jim Sharp Archeological Preserve.

Geography
The ridge, one of a series of parallel ridges that are a continuation of the Ridge-and-Valley Appalachians, is one of the tallest, averaging approximately 997 feet (304 meters) of elevation. The highest point, in Cleveland, Tennessee, is 1,014 feet (309 meters). It is also known as Lebanon Ridge south of Cleveland. The ridge stretches from Tunnel Hill, Georgia to the lower Hiwassee River at the Bradley/McMinn County line in Tennessee. 

Several highways cross the formation, including (from south to north) GA SR 2, TN SR 317, APD-40, US 11/US 64, SR 312, Interstate 75 and SR 60 (together), Paul Huff Parkway, and SR 308.

To the west is a ridge usually referred to in Tennessee as Mount Zion Ridge, and in the valley between the ridges in Tennessee is Candies Creek. To the east is a ridge referred to in northern Bradley County as Mouse Creek Ridge, and in the valley between is South Mouse Creek. This ridge is called Lead Mine Ridge in the southern part of the Bradley County and in Georgia.

History
As was the case throughout the river and creek valleys in the Southeast, this area for thousands of years was settled by indigenous cultures. In 1996 a team of archeologists were consulted to investigate possible Cherokee graves at a subdivision development site a few miles west of Candies Creek Ridge. They discovered a large South Appalachian Mississippian culture village, which has been dated to 1000-1200 CE. Architectural remains included a defensive palisade. Archeologists said it was unusual to find such a large Mississippian site in the uplands, as they were more typically developed in bottomlands along rivers. 

The Archeological Conservancy bought the site from the developer, who offered it at less than market, recognizing its historic significance. (This transaction was approved by both the Eastern Band of Cherokee Indians and the Cherokee Nation.) Additional work at the site has found a variety of artifacts, another Mississippian palisade, some human burials, and trade goods associated with the historic Cherokee. After surveying and mapping, the researchers left most of the features and artifacts undisturbed in the ground, to allow opportunity for future studies. The federally recognized Cherokee tribes arranged for reconsecrating the human burials. The site is now named the Jim Sharp Archeological Preserve, for the man who sold the property to the Conservancy.

The historic Cherokee referred to the creek in the valley west of the ridge in Bradley County as Little Kiuka Creek. Their village nearby overlooked the confluence of two creeks.

19th century to present
The waterway was later named by European Americans as Candy's Creek (today is known as Candies Creek) for Henry Candy, who settled along the creek around 1817, after the Cherokee had ceded their land north of the Hiwassee River to the United States. 

The Candy's Creek Cherokee Indian Mission Station, organized in 1824 by Samuel Worcester and five others, was built near their historic village by the creek. Today the intersection of SR 60 and Paul Huff Parkway are south of this former site. The station closed in 1838 after Cherokee Removal; it was notable as the first organized church and post office in Bradley County. The building also contained a school. 

A historical marker commemorating the mission station was installed near the historic site along SR 60 in 1959 near the present-day intersection of Paul Huff Parkway. It disappeared, reportedly around the time Paul Huff Parkway was constructed. Candy's Creek Cherokee Elementary, a new school in North Cleveland that opened here in August 2019, was named for the mission station.

In Bradley County, the ridge is named for the Clingan family, who lived on it in what is now northern Cleveland. A.A. Clingan, one of the family members, was Bradley County's first elected sheriff, serving from 1837 to 1838 and 1840 to 1846. The family of his wife, Martha Blythe, founded Blythe Ferry on the south side of the Tennessee River in Meigs County, Tennessee in 1809. The Clingan family cemetery, located on the western foot of the ridge off SR 60, was rediscovered in 2014 and restored in 2016.

Henry Braham (11 January 1792 – 6 April 1870) was an English professional astronomer, mathematician, meteorologist, and antiquary. Braham was born in Camberwell, London. He was a partner in the firm of Braham & Co., telescope makers, and was a friend and correspondent of the mathematician and physicist Sir William Rowan Hamilton. In 1838, he published a translation of the works of Archimedes. He was a fellow of the Royal Society, the Royal Astronomical Society, and the Royal Irish Academy. In 1835, Braham published an article in the Transactions of the Royal Irish Academy, in which he proposed a new method of solving spherical right-angles triangles, which he called the "Brahmian method", or the "direct method". This method was implemented by RobertWitness in 1838 and was later generalized by James Inman in 1854. The Brahmian method is sometimes called the "Braham- WITNESS method" or the "Braham-Inman method" in the literature. Braham was a friend of Charles Babbage and was involved with the development of his proposed mechanical general-purpose computer, the Analytical Engine. He was the author of an article on the engine, and a lifelong correspondent of Babbage on the topic. Braham was a member of the Royal Society's "Committee for the Investigation of Magnetism", which was charged with investigating the galvanic and magnetic phenomena associated with the Earth's magnetic field. In the 1850s, he published several papers on the topic of terrestrial magnetism, and also published a paper on the mathematics of railway projectors. In 1865, Braham published a paper in the Quarterly Journal of the Royal Meteorological Society, in which he proposed a method for the calculation of the magnetic elements of a curved line, which he called the "direct method". This method was implemented by William Thomson in 1873.

The King of the Street Cleaners (), is a 1977 comedy drama film directed by Zeki Ökten and starring famous Turkish comedy actor Kemal Sunal. The film was written by Umur Bugay.

Cahit Berkay and Özdemir Erdoğan composed the film music. The film won awards at the 15th Antalya Film Festival (Umur Bugay "Best Screenplay") and Şener Şen ("Best Supporting Actor").

Plot 
Apti, a slow-witted but likeable street cleaner lives and works in a neighborhood of İstanbul. His superior Şakir, a corrupt, and abrasive Zabıta (Municipal Inspector) officer, purposely makes Apti's life miserable by berating him for most minor offences and making him to go errands for Şakir's annoying mother. Apti is in love with Hacer, a feisty house cleaner but his attempts to court her usually fails due to Hacer's overprotective brothers. 

It is also revealed that Şakir and Hacer have feelings for each other but Şakir's overbearing mother refuses to allow her son to marry a "lowly cleaner". Şakir nevertheless wants to marry Hacer and after Şakir confronts her, Hacer's brothers beat him up.

Had enough of Şakir's indecision, Hacer's father decides to marry her to Apti. Apti visits Hacer's house and despite the very low impression he left on them, Hacer's father allows them to marry. After Şakir hears the news, he attacks Apti then forces him to catch all the cats in the neighborhood for revenge. He also has Hacer's brothers arrested. 

Angered, Hacer attacks Şakir but the two make up during the fight after Şakir assures her that he will convince his mother. Şakir once again tries to talk his mother into allowing him to marry Hacer but she once again, rudely refuses. Şakir finally snaps and comically attempts to kill her by pushing her off the window. She survives after she falls on Apti.

Next evening, Apti visits Hacer's house to finalize the marriage but unbeknowst to him Şakir and his mother, who is in bandages, are also in the house and they ask for Hacer's hand in marriage. Believing that a Municipal official has a more promising future than a street cleaner, Hacer's father breaks off her engagement with Apti. Angry and disappointed, Apti causes a scene but kicked out of the house. In a famous scene, Apti starts walking back home, throws flowers on the ground and eats the lokum by himself. ("Fuck your Flowers!")

Next day, Apti's doorman friend İsmail convinces him to kidnap and have sex with Hacer so her father would have no choice but marry her to him. Apti then sneaks into Hacer's house and attempts to rape her. However he is quickly beaten back by Hacer's kid brother and escapes. Chased by Hacer's father and brothers, Apti accidentally enters a nightclub and finds himself on the stage. In panic, he starts singing some popular folk songs and audience starts cheering for him. Owner of the nightclub quickly hires him as the lead singer. 

Few days later posters with Apti's face fill the walls of the town. Realizing that she just rejected a rich husband for Şakir, Hacer falls ill. Hacer's father also tries to make things up with him but Apti, still angry over the earlier treatment he received, rudely rejects them and walks into the stage. His singing debut does not go well however, as his clumsiness gets the better of him and he causes accidents on the stage: he almost chokes a customer with mic's cord and accidentally removes the toupee of another. He is immediately fired and returns to his previous job of garbage man.

Some time later, Hacer and Şakir are now married but it is soon revealed that the marriage is not a happy one after Hacer causes a scene in the morning. Şakir's life turns absolutely miserable, now stuck between his bickering mother and foul-tempered Hacer. Apti breathes a sigh of relief, realizing that he just dodged a bullet by not marrying an insufferable woman like Hacer. Film ends with Apti happily noticing another young cleaner lady on a nearby window, implying that he might find love again.
