Christian Basso (born 25 June 1966) is an Italian actor, film director, producer, and writer. Basso was born in Rome, Italy. He began his acting career in the theater, and later appeared in more than 60 films. He is best known for his performance as Paolo in the Academy Award-winning film Life Is Beautiful (1997), directed by Vincenzo Favio, for which he was nominated for a David di Donatello for Best Actor. In 1996, Basso won the David di Donatello for Best Supporting Actor for his performance in Il cielo è sempre più blu. In 2008, he was nominated for the David di Donatello for Best Supporting Actor for his performance in L'Auberge Espagnole. Basso wrote the screenplay for the film Un giorno perfetto, which was nominated for a David di Donatello for Best Film. In 2015, he was nominated for the David di Donatello for Best Supporting Actor for his performance in Quo Vado? Basso was also nominated for the David di Donatello for Best Supporting Actor for his performance in Illegittimo, in 2016. Basso's film Illegittimo was nominated for the David di Donatello for Best Film, in 2016.

Ryan Amador (born June 13, 1992) is an American soccer player who currently plays for San Diego Loyal in the USL Championship. Amador spent his early career in the North American Soccer League, playing for the Tampa Bay Rowdies and the San Francisco Deltas. Amador made his professional debut for the Tampa Bay Rowdies on April 18, 2015, in a 0–0 draw against the Fort Lauderdale Strikers. Amador scored his first professional goal on September 13, 2015, in a 2–1 victory against the Edmonton Eskimos. Amador was released by the Tampa Bay Rowdies on November 16, 2015. Amador signed with the San Francisco Deltas on December 14, 2015. Amador made his debut for the Deltas on April 1, 2016, in a 2–1 defeat against the Sacramento Republic. Amador scored his first goal for the Deltas on May 7, 2016, in a 2–0 victory against the Vancouver Whitecaps FC 2. Amador made his playoff debut for the Deltas on October 23, 2016, in a 3–2 victory against the Tulsa Roughnecks. Amador was released by the San Francisco Deltas on November 7, 2016. Amador signed with the San Diego Loyal on December 6, 2017.

McCormick-Stillman Railroad Park is a  railroad park located in Scottsdale, Arizona It features a  gauge railroad, a Magma Arizona Railroad locomotive, a railroad museum, three model railroad clubs and a  gauge live steam railroad.

History
In 1967, the Fowler McCormicks donated  of McCormick Ranch to the City of Scottsdale stipulating that it be used as a park for all people to enjoy. The son of Anne and James Stillman, Guy Stillman, assembled his  gauge narrow gauge railroad replica in the property. He called it the "Paradise & Pacific Railroad" and was offered to the city in 1971. The U.S. Marines, with the backing of Senator Barry Goldwater, contributed to the railroad's expansion by supplying manpower for the laying of tracks. The park officially opened on October 4, 1975. Originally named McCormick Railroad Park, it was renamed McCormick-Stillman Railroad Park in 1996 in recognition of its founder, Guy Stillman. 

The City of Scottsdale sold  of the donated land to a developer several years ago.

Attractions

 Paradise & Pacific Railroad: Created by Stillman, the Paradise & Pacific Railroad is the oldest and the first attraction to operate in the park. This  gauge ridable miniature railway has a route of  of track and  of sidetrack, including two trestles, a tunnel, 2 water tanks, loading platform, turntable, two crossing signals and an extensive train shed and workshop. The railroad has three steam locomotives (two operational), three diesel locomotives, two cabooses, a coach car, a trolley car (in the model RR building), three stock cars and a large number of gondola cars.
 Charro Carrousel: This merry-go-round was built in 1950 by The Allan Herschell Company and was purchased and restored by the Scottsdale Railroad & Mechanical Society.
 Playgrounds: The park has two children's playgrounds, one of them Southwestern-themed in an adobe construction.
 Statue of Bil Keane: Mr Keane was the creator of the newspaper comic The Family Circus.

Exhibits
 
 Magma Arizona Railroad Engine #6: This locomotive was built in 1907 by the Baldwin Locomotive Works and operated in the Magma Arizona Railroad for 54 years. It was purchased by the Scottsdale Railroad & Mechanical Society in 1977 and is the only Arizona & New Mexico engine remaining in Arizona.
 Railroad Museum
 Roald Amundsen Pullman car: Donated to the Scottsdale Railroad and Mechanical Society by Mr. and Mrs. Franz Talley, it is one of six cars built by the Pullman Company in 1928. It was constructed at a cost of $205,000 and used by Presidents Herbert Hoover, Franklin Roosevelt, Harry Truman, and Dwight Eisenhower. President Eisenhower used the car exclusively during the whistle-stop campaign in 1952. It was on this car on August 18, 1940, that President Roosevelt and Canadian Prime Minister W.L. McKenzie King met and mutually agreed to the Ogdensberg Declaration, a declaration that provided for the joint defense of North America in the event of foreign invasion. That was the beginning of the North American Air Defense, also known as N.O.R.A.D. It was donated to the park in 1971.
 The Swiss Railway Clock: The clock was donated to the McCormick-Stillman Railroad Park by the City of Interlaken, Switzerland. This was done in commemoration of the sister cities partnership of the cities of Interlaken and Scottsdale. The Swiss Railway Clock was designed in 1944 by Hans Hilfiker and was used by the Swiss federal Railways as a station clock.
 Gabe Brooks Machine Shop: Built in 1930 by Gabe Brooks, who was considered the finest machinist in the United States during World War II. It still contains original machining equipment.
 Navajo Hogans: These are two of the only three Indian hogans that exist off the Northern Arizona Navajo Indian Reservation. The other hogan is located at the Heard Museum in Phoenix, Arizona.
 Xeriscape Arboretum: This desert arboretum has been growing since 1980 and has different species of plants from different regions.
 The Antique Railroad Bell: This bell was presented to Henry S. Sturgis in 1958. Mr. Sturgis was the vice president of the First National Bank who played an active part in railroad finances. He served as chairman of the executive committee of the Erie Railroad.

Railroad clubs

Scottsdale Live Steamers: The Scottsdale Live Steamers is a  gauge miniature railroad which surrounds the Xeriscape Arboretum and was the former home of the Maricopa Live Steamers. The track is  long and has a loading platform, a train shed, a transfer table, a tunnel and one trestle. The layout has two main loops: the outer loop passes over the tunnel and the inner through it.

The following three railroad clubs are housed in the McCormick Ranch Bunkhouse:
 Scottsdale Model Railroad Historical Society: This club features a HO scale model train layout.
 Paradise & Pacific Tinplate Club: This club features an O scale model train layout.
 Sun-N Sand Club: This club features an N scale model train layout.

Clams casino is a clam "on the halfshell" dish with breadcrumbs and bacon. Green peppers are also a common ingredient.

It originated in Rhode Island in the United States. It is often served as an appetizer in New England and is served in variations nationally.

Ingredients
The dish uses littlenecks or cherrystone clams. Other basic ingredients include butter, peppers, bacon and garlic. Worcestershire sauce, salt and pepper, white wine, lemon juice, and shallots or onion are also used. Tabasco sauce is sometimes added, and parsley is sometimes used as a garnish.

Preparation

The clams, bacon, and other ingredients are cooked in various ways depending on the recipe, and then added with breading to half the clam shell and baked or broiled (grilled from above) to a golden brown.

There are many variations on the dish, but the constant factor is the bacon: "Bacon remains the major key to its success", with some chefs recommending smoked bacon for its salty flavor and others advocating an unsmoked variety.

History
According to legend, the recipe for clams casino was originally developed in 1917 in the Little Casino in Narragansett, Rhode Island, by a maître d'hôtel for a woman of means wanting something special for her guests. Good Housekeeping Great American Classics attributes the dish to Mrs. Paran Stevens and maître d'hôtel Julius Keller. She named the dish after the hotel, and word and popularity of the dish has since spread across the United States, including New Orleans, where oysters are substituted for clams. Clams casino remains a very popular dish in Rhode Island, "appearing on almost every menu".

The Central Park Casino was offering "soft clams a la Casino" as early as 1900.

According to Merrill Shindler, "in the first decades of this century [the 20th], if a restaurant wanted to be noted, it came up with a dish that involved the baking of shellfish". While there was a profusion of this type of menu offering (often with the meat taken out of the shell, prepared with sauce, and returned to the shell), clams casino and oysters Rockefeller "are among the few surviving dishes from the shellfish fad".

The dish is popular with Italian-Americans, having "a permanent spot on just about every trattoria menu" in Little Italy, Manhattan, and is considered an American classic. Clams casino is often served at Italian festivals and during the holidays in the United States.

Don't Be Tardy Don't Be Tardy is an American reality television series that premiered on September 15, 2008, on the Bravo cable network. The series is the first of threespin-offs of the network's hit series The Real Housewives of Atlanta. The series originally chronicled the life of Atlanta socialite and philanthropist Kim Zolciak as she and her family adjust to their new life in the suburbs, after her husband, NFL player Kroy Biermann, purchases a home. The series was originally titled The Real Housewives of Atlanta: Don't Be Tardy. In June 2010, it was announced that the series would be renewed for a third season, and would be undergoing a complete overhaul, with Kim Zolciak being the only original cast member to return. The series was subsequently renamed Don't Be Tardy, and began chronicling the life of Zolciak and her family after they moved back to the city. The third season premiered on September 20, 2010. In October 2011, the series was renewed for a fourth season, which premiered on November 4, 2011. In June 2012, the series was renewed for a fifth season, which premiered on November 3, 2012. In May 2013, the series was renewed for a sixth season, which premiered on November 2, 2013. In May 2014, the series was renewed for a seventh season, which premiered on November 8, 2014. In June 2015, the series was renewed for an eighth season, which premiered on November 7, 2015. In June 2016, the series was renewed for a ninth season, which premiered on November 6, 2016. In June 2017, the series was renewed for a tenth season, which premiered on November 5, 2017. In June 2018, the series was renewed for an eleventh season, which premiered on November 4, 2018. In June 2019, the series was renewed for a twelfth season, which premiered on November 3, 2019. In June 2020, the series was renewed for a thirteenth season, which premiered on November 2, 2020. In June 2021, the series was renewed for a fourteenth season, which premiered on November 1, 2021. In June 2022, the series was renewed for a fifteenth season, which premiered on October 31, 2022. The series has been executive produced by Andy Cohen, Michael Davies, Real Housewives of Atlanta executive producer Laura Chang, Paul Osborne, and Joseph LaBracio. The series was shot by Ryan Horrigan and narrated by Kroy Biermann. The series premiere was watched by 1.29 million total viewers, making it the highest-rated non-spinoff premiere in the franchise's history, and the second-highest rated cable television program of the day, behind the series premiere of The Real Housewives of New York City: The Fourth Season. The second season premiere was watched by 1.81 million total viewers, making it the series' highest rated episode since the first season premiere. The third season premiere was watched by 1.60 million total viewers. The fourth season premiere was watched by 1.77 million total viewers. The fifth season premiere was watched by 1.45 million total viewers. The sixth season premiere was watched by 1.35 million total viewers. The seventh season premiere was watched by 1.41 million total viewers. The eighth season premiere was watched by 1.51 million total viewers. The ninth season premiere was watched by 1.37 million total viewers. The tenth season premiere was watched by 1.27 million total viewers. The eleventh season premiere was watched by 1.11 million total viewers. The twelfth season premiere was watched by 1.41 million total viewers. The thirteenth season premiere was watched by 1.38 million total viewers. The fourteenth season premiere was watched by 1.44 million total viewers. The fifteenth season premiere was watched by 1.05 million total viewers.

Syed Arshad Ali (born 27 January 1943) is a Pakistani American writer, analyst, and scholar who has written extensively on the subjects of international relations, foreign policy, and defense and security issues. He has also written books and articles on the subjects of Islam and the West, and the history of science. Syed Arshad Ali was born in 1943 in Lahore, Punjab, in a literary and academic family. His father, Syed Zafar Ali, was a civil servant and his mother, Zareen Hussain, was a housewife. He was the second of their three children. After completing his early education at the Lawrence College, Ghora Gali, Murree (now in Pakistan), he went to the United States for higher studies. He attended the University of Washington in Seattle, and graduated with a bachelor's degree in political science in 1965. He then attended the School of Advanced International Studies (SAIS) at the Johns Hopkins University in Washington, D.C., where he received his master's degree in 1968 and his Ph.D. in 1972, both in international relations. After completing his education, Syed Arshad Ali began his career as an academic and writer. He worked as an assistant professor at the University of Wisconsin–Madison from 1973 to 1976, and then as an associate professor at the University of South Carolina from 1976 to 1982. In 1982, he joined the Foreign Service of Pakistan and served as an advisor on international affairs to the Chief Minister of Punjab, and then as the director of the Foreign Publicity Bureau in the Ministry of Foreign Affairs in Islamabad. In 1985, he was appointed as the principal staff writer and then as the editor of the Foreign Office Journal. In 1988, Syed Arshad Ali was posted as the counselor in the Pakistan Embassy in Washington, D.C., where he served until 1991. He was then appointed as the ambassador of Pakistan to South Korea, and served in this position until 1994. He was also the ambassador to North Korea from 1992 to 1994. After retiring from the Foreign Service, Syed Arshad Ali resumed his academic and writing career. He worked as a research associate at the Center for International and Security Studies at the University of Maryland from 1995 to 1996, and then as a visiting professor at the School of Advanced International Studies at the Johns Hopkins University from 1996 to 1997. He also worked as a senior research fellow at the National Institute of Pakistan Studies at Quaid-i-Azam University, Islamabad, from 1996 to 1997. In 1997, Syed Arshad Ali moved to New York City and worked as a writer and editor for the United Nations Development Programme (UNDP) from 1997 to 2002. He then worked as a senior writer and editor for the United Nations Department of Economic and Social Affairs (DESA) from 2002 to 2006. He was also the editor of the United Nations Economic and Social Survey of Asia and the Pacific from 2002 to 2006. Syed Arshad Ali has written numerous books and articles on international relations, foreign policy, and defense and security issues. His books include Pakistan's Foreign Policy: An Introduction, International Relations: A New Perspective, Foreign Policy of Pakistan: An Insight, and Islam and the West: A Historical and Political Perspective. His articles have been published in various newspapers and journals, including The New York Times, The Washington Post, The Wall Street Journal, Foreign Policy, Foreign Affairs, and The Journal of International Affairs. Syed Arshad Ali has also written on the subjects of history and science. His books on these subjects include The Life and Times of Sir Syed Ahmed Khan, and Science and Society in Ancient Greece. In addition to his writing, Syed Arshad Ali has also served as a consultant to various organizations and governments, including the United Nations, the World Bank, the Asian Development Bank, and the governments of Pakistan, the United States, and South Korea.

Afrânio, full name Afrânio de Melo Santos, (born 1 January 1908 in Limeira, São Paulo, Brazil – died 3 June 1981 in Limeira), was a Brazilian writer, poet, journalist, and politician. Afrânio's parents were Adolfo de Melo Santos (born in Limeira) and Maria Augusta de Oliveira Santos (born in Cachoeira Paulista). He married Laura de Oliveira Peixoto, with whom he had three daughters: Laura, Luciana, and Sofia. After completing high school, Afrânio studied law, but did not complete his degree. He worked as a journalist, writing for the newspapers "O Estado de São Paulo", "O Diário de Notícias", and "Folha de São Paulo". Afrânio began his political career in 1945, when he was elected to the city council of Limeira. In 1947 he became the city's mayor, and in 1948 he was elected to the state legislature. In 1950 he became the state's vice governor, and in 1954 he was elected governor. He served as governor until 1959, and in 1960 he was elected to the federal Chamber of Deputies. In 1964 he was elected to the federal Senate, where he served until 1979. Afrânio was a member of the National Democratic Union (UDN) party, and he was also a member of the Chamber of Deputies' Communist Bloc. In his capacity as a politician, Afrânio was a strong supporter of the military regime that ruled Brazil from 1964 to 1985. Afrânio's first book, O Poço, was published in 1947. He published several other books of poetry, including O Descobrimento da Terra (1952), O Fósforo Eleitoral (1957), and Nova linha de propósito (1975). His works of fiction include O Tempo não passa (1961) and O Homem que não amava (1977). He also published a collection of essays, O nosso Brasil (1965).

Wilkinson County School District is a public school district based in Wilkinson County, Georgia, United States. The district includes Wilkinson County and Bleckley County and serves the communities of Abbeville, Allentown, Ashwood, Bartow, Bellville, Cadwell, Chester, Content, Dodge, Empire, Epworth, Fleming, Gordon, Greenpond, Hickory Flat, Ivey, Jackons, Java, Kennedy, Lancing, Martin, Meriwether, Mobley, Mount Carmel, New Center, North High Shoals, Oak Hill, Offerman, Palestine, Reddick, Reynolds, Round Oak, Shady Dale, Smithville, Tally, Toomsboro, Union Church, Vada, Victoria, Waldron, Willacoochee, and Wilkinson. There are a total of nine elementary schools, two middle schools, and one high school in the district. The schools are operated by the Wilkinson County School District. The district's nine elementary schools serve students in kindergarten through grade five. The schools are Bartow Elementary School in Bartow, Dodge Elementary School in Dodge, Gordon Elementary School in Gordon, Greenpond Elementary School in Greenpond, Lancing Elementary School in Lancing, Martin Elementary School in Martin, Meriwether Elementary School in Meriwether, Offerman Elementary School in Offerman, and Wilkinson Elementary School in Wilkinson. The district's two middle schools serve students in grades six through eight. The schools are Wilkinson Middle School in Wilkinson and Meriwether Middle School in Meriwether. The district's one high school serves students in grades nine through twelve. The school is Wilkinson County High School in Irwinton.

Huis Honselaarsdijk Huis Honselaarsdijk is a 16th-century farmhouse on the Honselaarsdijkseweg in the village of Honselersdijk in the municipality of Alphen aan den Rijn, province of South Holland, Netherlands. The farmhouse was built in 1597 on the Honselaarsdijkseweg, a dike that was raised in that same year to replace the so-called "Binnenwaterdijk" which had been built in 1570. The Binnenwaterdijk was an artificial dike along the "Binnenwater" (the present-day Waal), which had been created by the inhabitants of the "Binnenland" (the present-day Betuwe) to protect their land from flooding by the Meuse and the Waal. The Honselaarsdijkseweg formed the western boundary of the Binnenland. The farmhouse has a frontage of 30 metres (98 ft) and a depth of 15 metres (49 ft). The building is divided into three sections. The left section contains the oldest part of the farmhouse, dating from the early 17th century. The middle section dates from the late 17th century and the right section from the early 18th century. The façade of the house has many characteristics of the so-called "W pallasidam" style, a style that was popular in the 16th and 17th centuries in the Betuwe area. This style is named after the first known example, a farmhouse in Aschanspergerstraat in the city of Tiel. The W pallasidam style is characterized by the use of red brick, stone, and slate, and by dormer windows, lean-to roofs, and crow-stepped gables. In 1629, the farmhouse was extended to the left with a rectangular tower. The tower has a superstructure that is half as high as the length of the farm (15 metres (49 ft)). The function of the tower was to provide a better overview of the Binnenwater. In 1632, a side-wing was added to the west side of the farmhouse. In 1639, an chapel was built on the first floor of the tower. In 1655, the chapel was extended to the west with a choir and a sacristy. In 1675, the farmhouse was extended to the right with a semi-circle extension. In the 19th century, the chapel was restored and a gallery was added. In 1964, the choir was restored. In 1998, the interior of the chapel was restored.

The Monteregian Hills () is a linear chain of isolated hills in Montreal and Montérégie, between the Laurentians and the Appalachians.

Etymology
The first definition of the Monteregian Hills came about in 1903 when Montreal geologist Frank Dawson Adams began referring to Mount Royal (Latin, Mons Regius) and hills of similar geology in the Saint Lawrence Lowlands as the "Royal Mountains" (). Other hills in the chain included Mont Saint-Bruno, Mont Saint-Hilaire, Mont Saint-Grégoire, Mont Rougemont, Mont Yamaska, Mont Shefford, and Mont Brome.

It was only later that Mont Mégantic, the Oka Hills, as well as the Saint-André and d'Iberville formations, were added to the list.

Geology
Each hill in the chain consists of an erosional remnant of Cretaceous intrusive igneous rock and associated hornfels, which are more resistant to weathering than the surrounding sedimentary rock. All of the hills have dark-coloured mafic rock such as gabbro and essexite; some also have large areas of pulaskite, syenite, and other light-coloured rock.

The Monteregian Hills are part of the Great Meteor hotspot track, formed as a result of the North American Plate sliding westward over the long-lived New England hotspot, and are the eroded remnants of intrusive stocks. These intrusive stocks have been variously interpreted as the feeder intrusions of long extinct volcanoes, which would have been active about 125 million years ago, or as intrusives that never breached the surface in volcanic activity. The lack of an obvious track west of the Monteregian Hills may be due either to failure of the plume to penetrate the Canadian Shield, to the lack of recognizable intrusions, or to strengthening of the plume when it approached the Monteregian Hills region. However, there is evidence the hotspot track extends northwestwards, including epeirogenic uplift, mantle velocity anomalies and kimberlitic volcanic features (e.g. the Attawapiskat, Kirkland Lake and Lake Timiskaming kimberlite fields) that become older away from the Monteregian Hills.

The shallow, rocky sandy loam soils of the summits are mostly covered in forest. Where the underlying rock is rich in olivine, as over large areas of Mont Saint-Bruno and Mont Rougemont, these soils are classed as dystric brunisol. Podzol tends to develop over rock which lacks olivine, although many of these podzols lack an eluvial (Ae) horizon. Lower slopes are covered with aprons of gravel or sand. The sandy soils are usually podzols with classic Ae development; they often have subsoil hardpan and are undesirable for agriculture. The free-draining gravels are preferred for apple orchards, which grow in thermal belts where cold air can drain to the valley floor.

William Smith (loyalist) (January 17, 1756 – July 22, 1822) was a businessman, judge, politician, and statesman who helped found the state of Tennessee and signed the United States Constitution. Born in Elizabeth County, New Jersey, Smith attended the College of New Jersey (later Princeton) and graduated in 1774. He joined the Continental Army in 1775 and served as an officer in the New Jersey Line during the American Revolutionary War. He was taken prisoner by the British in the fall of 1776 and held in New York until he was paroled in June 1778. He rejoined the Continental Army in 1781 as a staff officer and served in that capacity until the close of the war. After the war, Smith moved to the newly established state of Tennessee and settled in Nashville, where he became a wealthy businessman and slaveholder. He was a member of the state constitutional convention in 1796, and he helped Tennessee become the 16th state in 1796. He also helped draft the constitution of the new state. In 1799, Smith was elected to the United States House of Representatives, where he served three terms. He was a member of the Federalist Party and an advocate of strong federal government. He also served as a state court judge and as a member of the Tennessee Supreme Court. In 1815, Smith was chosen by the Tennessee General Assembly to be one of the state's two United States Senators, along with Daniel Smith (no relation). The two senators served together from 1815 to 1821, when William Smith retired. He died the following year.

The United Kingdom Standard Industrial Classification of Economic Activities, or UKSIC, is a system for classifying industries by business activity. The UKSIC was developed by the Central Intelligence Agency (CIA) and is used by the Office for National Statistics (ONS) to categorise business establishments and other standard units, and is used by government departments and agencies for the collection, analysis, and publication of statistics. The UKSIC is a revision of the International Standard Industrial Classification of All Economic Activities (ISIC), and was developed by the UK to replace the ISIC with a classification more suited to the UK economy. It was first used for the 1981 Census of Production, but was also used for the 1981 Census of Population and Housing, to provide an industrial classification for establishments in the Census. The UKSIC was then used for the 1991 Census, and has been used for all Censuses since. The UKSIC is derived from the ISIC, and has the same basic structure of ten sections, each divided into divisions, each divided into groups, each divided into classes. The UKSIC has twenty-one divisions, seventy-one groups, and 180 classes. The UKSIC is the basis for the Business Structure Database (BSD), which is used to produce the Business Insights and Conditions Survey (BICS), which in turn is used to produce the Business Activity Index (BAI), the longest series of business activity measures covering the UK economy. The UKSIC has also been used to produce a classification of regions in the UK, the Regional Industrial Classification (RIC), which is used to produce regional economic data. In 2004, the UKSIC was replaced by the International Standard Industrial Classification of Economic Activities (ISIC) rev.4, but the ONS has continued to use the UKSIC for the convenience of users, and because the change to ISIC rev.4 caused breaks in some series. The UKSIC has therefore been retained for backward compatibility, and will continue to be used by the ONS for the foreseeable future. The UKSIC is used to classify units by business activity, but there are some units that are not classifiable, and these units are placed in the "Not Classifiable" class (value 9999). The UKSIC is a ten-digit code, and is hierarchical, with the rightmost digit having the lowest importance: 1. Activity 2. Sub-activity 3. Group 4. Class The first two digits give the section, the next two the division, the next two the group, and the last two the class. For example, 21.3.3.1 is the code for Oil and gas extraction, and 21.3.3.9 is the code for Support activities for oil and gas extraction. The UKSIC has ten sections, as follows: 01-09 Oil and gas extraction 09-16 Manufacturing 16-32 Construction 32-33 Wholesale and retail trade, except motor vehicles and motorcycles 33-40 Motor vehicle and motorcycle wholesale and retail 40-45 Business services 45-50 Financial services 50-53 Insurance services 53-59 Other financial services 59-60 Real estate, renting and business activities 60-62 Professional services 62-63 Health services 63-69 Education services 69-72 Social services 72-73 Personal services 73-76 Other services 76-99 Not classifiable The main changes from the ISIC are: The "agriculture" section has been collapsed into "oil and gas extraction", as the UK has a negligible amount of arable land. The "fishing" section has been collapsed into "manufacturing", as the UK has a negligible amount of coastline. "Fur farming" is collapsed into "manufacturing". "Salt extraction" is collapsed into "manufacturing". The "textile, leather, and clothing industries" are collapsed into "manufacturing". The "wood and wood products" section is collapsed into "construction". The "metal products" section is collapsed into "manufacturing". The "chemical industries" are collapsed into "manufacturing". The "plastics and rubber industries" are collapsed into "manufacturing". The "glass and ceramics industries" are collapsed into "manufacturing". The "power generation" section is collapsed into "manufacturing". The "diesel power generation" section is collapsed into "manufacturing". The "gas and steam power generation" section is collapsed into "manufacturing". The "hydro power generation" section is collapsed into "manufacturing". Extermination of Evil Extermination of Evil is a concept that was introduced into the Catholic Church by Pope Pius XII in the mid-20th century. The concept was further developed by the Second Vatican Council, in the 1960s. The idea is that since the Fall of Man, the world is in a fallen state, and must be restored to its original condition by an act of divine intervention. This intervention would be the final act in the drama of salvation, and would mark the end of the present world order, and the beginning of the eternal state. In the interim, the Church is called to be the "salt of the earth", the "light of the world", and to be the "seed of the kingdom". The concept is related to the idea of the "kingdom of God", which is also referred to as the "kingdom of heaven". The kingdom of God is the sphere of God's rule, and the concept has been developed in the New Testament, particularly by Jesus and the apostles. The idea of the kingdom of God has also been developed by the Catholic Church, particularly in the modern period. The concept of the kingdom of God is related to the idea of the "kingdom of Christ", which is the idea of the final state of the world, after the return of Christ and the final judgment. The concept of the kingdom of Christ is related to the idea of the "kingdom of the saints", which is the idea of the intermediate state, after the fall of the present world order, but before the final state. The concept of the kingdom of God is also related to the idea of the "kingdom of nature", which is the idea that the world is a kingdom, and that the world is a natural entity, which is governed by natural laws. The concept of the kingdom of nature is related to the idea of the "kingdom of grace", which is the idea that the world is a kingdom, and that the world is a supernatural entity, which is governed by supernatural laws.

Debra Elaine Houry is an American physician. She is the acting principal deputy director of the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, having formally served as the Director of	the	National Center	for	Injury Prevention and Control.

Early life and education
Houry was born and raised in Virginia. She completed her undergraduate degree at Emory University in 1994 with a double major in biology and philosophy. She also volunteered at Children's Healthcare of Atlanta at Egleston, worked as a house staff assistant at Grady Hospital. From Emory, Houry entered the dual medical degree and MPH program at Tulane University. Following this, she trained in emergency medicine at Denver Health Medical Center and received the Council of Emergency Medicine Residency Directors Resident Academic Achievement Award.

Career
Following her residency, Houry became an assistant professor of emergency medicine and of occupational and environmental health at Emory University, as well as associate director of Emory’s Center for Injury Control. While serving in this role, Houry was elected as the president of the Society for Academic Emergency Medicine.

In May 2013, Houry was selected as a Fellow in the Hedwig van Ameringen Executive Leadership in Academic Medicine program to prepare women for senior leadership roles in academic health institutions. The following year, she was appointed director of the National Center for Injury Prevention and Control (NCIPC) at the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC). During her tenure at the CDC, Houry oversaw the release of "Guidelines for Prescribing Opioids for Chronic Pain," which gives providers research and academic evidence for managing patient opioid prescriptions.

As a result of her research, Houry was elected a member of the National Academy of Medicine in 2019 and appointed acting principal deputy director of the CDC.

3rd Battalion, 9th Marines, was one of the most decorated infantry units in the history of the United States Marine Corps. The unit, based out of Marine Corps Base Camp Smedley D. Butler in Okinawa, Japan, was known for its lineage, as well as its fighting ability. Raised in 1917, the unit was deployed to the European theater of World War II, where it participated in several major battles, including the Battle of Iwo Jima, the Battle of Saipan, and the Battle of Tinian. The unit suffered heavy casualties during these battles, but it also earned a reputation for its determination and fighting ability. After the war, the unit was assigned to the 1st Marine Brigade, and it was deployed to the Korean War. The unit was one of the first Marine units to enter the war, and it suffered heavy casualties during the early stages of the war. However, the unit also played a major role in the Inchon Landing and the subsequent capture of Seoul. The unit was again deployed to the Vietnam War, and it was one of the first Marine units to enter the country. The unit suffered heavy casualties during the war, but it also earned a reputation for its fighting ability. In the late 1970s, the unit was assigned to the 3rd Force Service Support Group, and it was deployed to the Middle East, where it participated in the Iran hostage crisis. The unit was also deployed to the Gulf War, and it played a major role in the initial stages of the war. In the late 1990s, the unit was assigned to the 1st Marine Expeditionary Brigade, and it was deployed to the Taiwan Strait, where it participated in the Taiwan Strait crisis. The unit was again deployed to the Middle East, where it participated in the Iraq War. The unit played a major role in the initial stages of the war, and it suffered heavy casualties. In the late 2000s, the unit was assigned to the 3rd Force Service Support Group, and it was deployed to the Middle East, where it participated in the Afghanistan War.

Iain Edward O'Brien (born 10 July 1976) is a former New Zealand cricketer, who played 22 Tests, 10 ODIs and 4 T20Is. A pace bowler, he took 73 test wickets with best figures of 6 for 75 against West Indies in 2008. He played first class cricket for Wellington, the Leicestershire County Cricket Club and the Middlesex County Cricket Club.

He retired from all cricket in January 2012 due to chronic injury problems.

International career
O'Brien made his debut for the national side in March 2005 following injuries to senior international players, such as Shane Bond, Daryl Tuffey and Chris Martin but has been in and out of the side since to replace injured and retired players. He made his One Day International debut against the England cricket team, and had a tough debut, with figures of 1/59 in 6 overs.

However, between 2007 and 2009 he established himself in the Test side, and to a lesser extent, in the ODI side, particularly in the absence of Shane Bond. He was renowned as an 'into-the-wind' bowler who would often bowl at an end that did not offer favourable bowling conditions, especially at the Basin Reserve in Wellington, a ground renowned for strong winds. He enjoyed much success as a fast bowler for the Black Caps, with great performances against England in the UK, and against the West Indies cricket team at home, where he took his best Test figures of 6/75, in a spell of bowling regarded as one of the best in New Zealand. These performance have given him the reputation as a wicket-taking bowler, and being the go-to man for Daniel Vettori, the captain. He continued his consistent spells against the Australia cricket team, home and away, and for Wellington.

During New Zealand's tour of England in 2008, he had had similar success, taking 15 wickets at 26.80. Against the Pakistan cricket team at home in December 2009, he has become well known for bowling New Zealand to victory by taking 3 wickets while suffering from a broken thumb. In this same series, he took 6 wickets in the next game, including 4/66, dismissing the likes of Salman Butt, Imran Farhat and Misbah-ul-Haq.

In December 2009, he announced his decision to retire from international cricket to spend more time with his wife in England and this decision prompted Middlesex to sign O'Brien as their overseas player for the 2010 season.

Domestic career
He played at Leicestershire during the 2009 season as their overseas player, impressing by taking 21 first-class wickets at an average of 26.04 in a struggling team, including 9 wickets in a game where he took 6/39 on his birthday.

The 2010 season was a frustrating one for O'Brien, he took 23 wickets at an average of 27.30 for Middlesex in division two of the County championship. However, injury restricted his appearances for Middlesex to just seven championship matches, one CB40 match (in which he took 4–41 in 8 overs) & a single FP Twenty20 match.

Writing 
Part of O'Brien's appeal to fans is because of his writing, both for his own blog and for cricinfo. Rarely has an international cricketer been so candid about his own faults during his career. O'Brien's blog is not updated straight away after all games, but he generally talks about all games he has played in. It is a far cry from the ghost written columns of most cricketers, and has got him in hot water with NZC before. O'Brien really enjoys writing and has already been a special guest writer in the book Ashes 09: When Freddie Became Jesus and is contributing to a book on New Zealand Indoor Cricket as well.

O'Brien also is an occasional contributor to Test Match Sofa – an online alternative cricket commentary service as well as BBC and Sky cricket commentary teams.

The 2021 The Spring League season is the sixth and final season of The Spring League. This is the only season to feature eight teams in two divisions: the North Division in Indianapolis, Indiana and the South Division in Houston, Texas.

The Linemen beat the Jousters 26–23 in the first and only Mega Bowl, and won the TSL championship. Linemen QB Ryan Willis won both final and regular season MVP awards.

In November the league was sued for failing to pay $1.4 million in bills during a nine-week stay in Indianapolis, for the use of Lucas Oil Stadium ($235,000 for six games) and hotel stay ($1.1 million for 4,740 room nights). Also, Butler University sued TSL for about $33,000 in unpaid bills, for its use of the Sellick Bowl for practices.

On February 18, 2022, Woods stated that TSL was "no longer operational."

Format and rules
The 2021 season kicked off on Thursday, May 6 and was divided to two divisions that played in hub cities: The North Division in Indianapolis and the South Division in Houston. Each division champion will advance to the 2021 TSL Championship game that will take place on June 19.

The 2021 season featured new rules that aim to improve the game, specifically introducing a new overtime format, the “spot and choose” rule, which means that the team that wins the overtime coin toss was to pick the spot of the ball, while the other team would choose whether to play offense or defense. A similar rule was proposed by the Baltimore Ravens after the 2020 NFL season.

Players
The player participation fee for the 2021 season was $2,000, with exception for players who had been in an NFL training camp within the past three years, who were not required to pay. According to league CEO Brian Woods, the preference was for TSL coaches, players and staff to be vaccinated for COVID-19 before the season begins. If not, they were asked for proof of a negative test upon arrival and were subject to daily testing thereafter.

Every team started the season with 35 to 41 players roster, and were allowed to make changes throughout the season.

Tryouts
In March 2021, TSL announced that they reached a partnership agreement with Stephen Austin's American National Combines, and were holding tryouts for the first time in league history. They also declared that players who were on an active roster during TSL's fall 2020 season are not required to participate in the tryouts.

Notable players
On April 16, the first player for the 2021 TSL season was announced, as former Syracuse and Dallas Renegades QB Eric Dungey confirmed he will play for the Blues, although he was later accepted tryout invite to Cincinnati Bengals rookie minicamp, and was later signed to their 90-men roster. On later date the league revealed the other Quarterbacks who will play, including seven-year NFL veteran Ryan Mallett (Generals), former XFL players Luis Perez (Jousters), Brandon Silvers (Conquerors), and Nick Fitzgerald (Sea Lions), and three former P5 colleges QB's Brian Lewerke (Alphas), Keller Chryst (Aviators), and Ryan Willis (Linemen).

On May 12, after the first week was concluded, TSL announced that former Super Bowl champion Brandon Marshall was joining the Generals after strong showing at HUB Football camp, but before the Generals game on week 2 it was revealed that Marshall decided to pursue NFL opportunities instead of joining the league.

39 players with XFL experience were on TSL rosters, and 19 players who participated in the Fan Controlled Football 2021 season (Joseph Putu, Roman Tatum, David Gilbert, Kwadarrius Smith, Curtis Collins, Dominique Martin, Jaylen Flye-Sadler, EJ price, JaMichael Edwards Lott, Deondre Francois, Lawerence Keys, Dwayne Wallace, Donald Boone, Ladarius Galloway, Andrew Jamiel, KaVontae Turpin, Saige Young, Cecil Cherry and Antonio Dawkins).

The league also featured three international players: Kicker Tadhg Leader (Aviators) from the Republic of Ireland, Defensive Linemen Orlovicius Laurynas (Blues) from Lithuania and Wide receiver James Tyrrell from Canada.

Other noteworthy players
  
Alphas: Vad Lee, Brian Lewerke, Jawill Davis, Kristjan Sokoli, Christian DiLauro, Gabe Holmes, Azeem Victor and Mazzi Wilkins, while Brady White joined before week 4.

Aviators: Keller Chryst, Romar Morris, Malcolm Pridgeon, Lavon Hooks, Mark McLaurin and Irish-born American rugby union player Tadhg Leader, while Vad Lee joined from the "Alphas" before week 5.

Blues: Eric Dungey, Deondre Francois, Fabian Guerra, Davion Davis, Hakeem Valles, David Grinnage, Denzel Rice and Xavier Perston.

Conquerors: Brandon Silvers, Kevin Anderson, Cameron Jefferson, Anthony Morris, Robenson Therezie, Matt Seybert, Prince Charles Iworah and Vinny Papale (son of Vince Papale), while Ahmad Gooden joined before week 2.

Generals: Ryan Mallett, Case Cookus, Devin Gray, Jordan Suell, Sal Cannella, Ethan Westbrooks, Blair Brown, Carroll Phillips, Kamilo Tongamoa, Nevelle Clarke, Malik Gant and Lirim Hajrullahu.

Jousters: Luis Perez, Drew Anderson, Lavon Coleman, De'Lance Turner, De’Quan Hampton, Damore'ea Stringfellow, Donnie Ernsberger, Tejan Koroma, Damien Mama, Bunmi Rotimi, Tomasi Laulile, Christian Sam, Nyles Morgan, Derrick Jones, A. J. Hendy, Shalom Luani, Joshua Simmons, William Likely, Ricky Aguayo, Manoa Pikula and Colton Schmidt.

Linemen: Ryan Willis, Reece Horn, Frederick Mauigoa, Elijah Qualls and Jordan Wyatt, while Damon Sheehy-Guiseppi joined before week 6.

Sea Lions: Nick Fitzgerald, Matt Jones (released after week 1), Adam Choice, Kendrick Rogers, Nick Buchanan, Shaneil Jenkins, Levonta Taylor, John Baron II and DeAndre Johnson from Last Chance U' second season, while Jordan Mills and Robert Myers joined before week 2, and Garrett Hartley joined before week 4.

Coaches
For the 2021 spring season TSL featured a mix of returning coaches from previous seasons and former college football college or minor leagues HC's. Terry Shea (Aviators), Ted Cottrell (Blues), Jerry Glanville (Conquerors) and defending champion Bart Andrus (Generals) returned to their 2020 Fall season teams, while Peter Vaas (Alphas) and Kevin Gilbride (Jousters) replaced Steve Fairchild and Chuck Bresnahan (respectively). Hal Mumme was appointed as the "Linemen" head coach, while Mike Riley was first announced as the "Sea Lions" coach, but it was later changed to Larry Kirksey. Shea is the only one that coached in every TSL season.

Some of the more recognizable assistants were Paul Spicer (DC, Sea Lions), AJ Smith (OC, Conquerors), Derrius Bell (DC, Blues), Jay Hayes (DC, Jousters), Jeff Reinebold (DC/STC, Generals), Dale Carlson (WR, Linemen), Blake Brockermeyer (OL, Sea Lions) and Johnnie Mack (RB, Blues), while QB Stan Bedwell was a player-coach for the Linemen.

Teams
The 2021 season featured two new teams: The Linemen and Sea Lions, which were added to the six returning teams from the 2020 fall season. 

{{small| Week 6 games were played at the Bud and Jackie Sellick Bowl.}}

Games
The games will be played on Thursdays, Fridays and Saturdays, and for the first time, one game every week will be broadcast on FOX (including the championship game), while all other games will be broadcast on FS1 and FS2. OvertimeStandings

Future plans
On June 3, 2021, Woods announced that he had acquired the remaining extant trademarks of the United States Football League with intent of launching a USFL-branded league in 2022, with Fox Sports remaining as a partner in the new USFL. What will become of The Spring League remains an unresolved question, but according to the initial announcement TSL will continue, probably as a scouting showcase or developmental operation.

Ivan Sertić (born 27 February 1985) is a retired Croatian football forward.

Career
Sertić start to play football in NK Rijeka. Between 2001 and 2003 Sertić is a part of Croatia national under-17 football team.

In 2005, he is loaned in NK Novalja for six months.

In season 2007/08 the forward played for NK Pomorac.
In summer 2008 he signed a contract with Bulgarian Belasitsa Petrich. Sertić made his official debut for the Bulgarian club on 10 August 2008 in a match against CSKA Sofia as a 74th min substitute. On 8 November 2008 he scored his first goal for Belasitsa in a match against Litex Lovech.

In July 2015, Sertić returned to his former club HNK Orijent 1919. Two years later, in June 2017, he announced his retirement from football.

Arthur L. Wagner (November 27, 1885 – February 6, 1961) was an American lawyer, soldier, politician, and businessman. Wagner was born in Lincoln, Nebraska, and attended the University of Nebraska, where he received his bachelor's degree in 1908 and his master's degree in 1909. He received his law degree from the University of Nebraska College of Law in 1911. He was a practicing attorney in Nebraska from 1911 to 1912, when he moved to Pierre, South Dakota, to become an attorney for the South Dakota Railroad Commission. He later became general counsel for the commission. Wagner served in the United States Army during World War I, attaining the rank of captain. After the war, he returned to Nebraska and became a member of the Nebraska House of Representatives from 1939 to 1940. In 1940, he was elected to the Nebraska State Senate, where he served until his death. Wagner was a member of the Republican Party. He was a delegate to the Republican National Convention in 1940, 1944, and 1948. In 1952, he was an alternate delegate to the Republican National Convention. Wagner died in Lincoln, Nebraska, on February 6, 1961, at the age of 75.

Castle Mountain is a high, prominent mountain in the Alaska Range of Alaska. It is located just north of the Richardson Highway and the town of Glennallen, about half way between Anchorage and Valdez. The mountain is named for the castle-like shape of its summit. The local name was first noted in 1898 by the U.S. Geological Survey. The mountain is also known as "The Castle" or "castle peak". The Castle Mountain area is a popular destination for hiking, climbing, and other outdoor activities. The mountain itself is a challenging climb due to its steep, exposed flanks. The first ascent was made in 1930 by M. Agee, T. Agee, and D. Mahre. The Alaska Range is a large mountain system that extends for hundreds of miles across the state of Alaska. The range is home to some of the highest and most prominent mountains in the state, including Denali, Mount Foraker, and Mount Spurr. Castle Mountain is one of the many notable peaks in the range. Henry Franklin Sloan (February 15, 1885 – August 9, 1965) was an American football player and coach, and a college athletics administrator. He served as the head football coach at the University of Minnesota from 1913 to 1914, at the University of Michigan from 1915 to 1921, and at the University of Oregon from 1922 to 1932, compiling a career college football record of 104–37–11. Sloan's teams won Big Ten Conference championships in 1917, 1918, and 1921 and Pacific Coast Conference championships in 1925 and 1926. Sloan was born in 1885 in Jamaica, Iowa, and grew up in nearby Tabor, Iowa. He attended the University of Iowa, where he played halfback and served as the captain of the Iowa Hawkeyes football team in 1905. He graduated from Iowa in 1906 and then attended the University of Illinois, where he earned a master's degree in 1908. Sloan began his coaching career at the University of Illinois in 1908 as the assistant football coach under John Macklin. He then served as the head football coach at the University of South Dakota from 1909 to 1912, leading the South Dakota Coyotes to a 16–4–2 record in four years. In 1913, he was hired as the head coach at the University of Minnesota, where he led the Golden Gophers to a 7–0 record and a Big Ten Conference championship in 1913. The following year, the Gophers finished 8–1, with a 4–1 record in conference play, but they did not repeat as conference champions. Sloan was hired as the head football coach at the University of Michigan in 1915. He remained the head coach at Michigan for seven years from 1915 to 1921, during which he led the Wolverines to a 39–13–3 record, including a 3–1 record in Big Ten Conference play in 1917 and a 5–1 record in conference play in 1921. His teams also won or shared Big Ten championships in 1917 and 1918. His overall record at Michigan was 39–13–3, giving him a .750 winning percentage. Sloan was the head coach at the University of Oregon from 1922 to 1932. He led the Ducks to a 42–36–11 record, including a 7–3 record in 1925 and a 6–0–1 record in 1926. His teams won the Pacific Coast Conference (PCC) championship in 1925 and 1926 and played in the Rose Bowl in 1925, losing to the University of Pennsylvania, 14–3. Sloan's overall record as a college football coach was 104–37–11. He had a .729 winning percentage as a coach, including a .759 winning percentage in conference games. Sloan also served as the athletic director at Oregon from 1923 to 1932 and at the University of Missouri from 1933 to 1941. He later worked as a professor of physical education at Oregon from 1941 to 1945. Sloan died in his home in Eugene, Oregon, in 1965 at age 80.

Eugene de Kock (born 16 June 1941) is a South African convicted of murder, crimes against humanity, and other charges in connection with the death of anti-apartheid activist Steve Biko. de Kock was born in South Africa. He became involved with the police force in 1968 and joined the security branch in 1976. He became the head of the security branch in 1994. In 1998, he was found to have been personally responsible for the death of Steve Biko, the founder of the Black Consciousness Movement, in 1977. de Kock was also found guilty of having personally killed, or having participated in the killing of, many other anti-apartheid activists. In 1998, de Kock was sentenced to life in prison, and was ordered to serve at least 25 years. He served his sentence at the Cullinan Prison, and was released on parole in 2014. In 2002, the Truth and Reconciliation Commission (TRC) was established to investigate and report on the actions of the apartheid state, and to recommend compensation and other measures. de Kock was asked to appear before the TRC, and did so in 2003. The TRC Report recommended that he should not be prosecuted for his crimes, and that he should be granted immunity from prosecution. However, in 2007, a group of lawyers challenged the TRC's recommendation on the basis that it had been inconsistent with the law. In 2013, the High Court of South Africa ruled that the TRC's recommendation was inconsistent with the law, and that de Kock should face a new trial. The ruling was appealed to the Constitutional Court of South Africa, which confirmed the High Court's decision in 2014. In 2015, de Kock was granted a stay of prosecution, pending the outcome of an application to the Constitutional Court for a permanent stay of prosecution. In 2018, the Constitutional Court refused de Kock's application for a permanent stay of prosecution. In 2020, the High Court of South Africa ruled that the TRC's recommendation was consistent with the law, and that de Kock should face a new trial. The ruling was appealed to the Constitutional Court of South Africa, which confirmed the High Court's decision in 2021. In 2022, de Kock was granted a stay of prosecution, pending the outcome of an application to the Constitutional Court for a permanent stay of prosecution.

History of the ambulance The earliest known use of the ambulance is found in the writings of the ancient Greek historian, Thucydides. He describes the use of "a cart with a lifting gangway" for the transport of the wounded from the field of battle in the year 420 BC. In the early Roman Empire, the Republic's army had a designated "Vehiculum Sacrum" or "sacred vehicle" for the transport of the dead and the wounded. The Roman army used horses and mules for the transport of the sick and the wounded. They also used oxen and slaves to pull the "Vehiculum Sacrum". The "Vehiculum Sacrum" was a common sight on the battlefields of the ancient world. In the medieval period, the church used the "Arca" or "box" to transport the relics of saints. The "Arca" was also used to transport the wounded. In the 16th century, the ambulance was used to transport the wounded from the battlefields of the Thirty Years' War. The first organized group of ambulance drivers was the "Holy Ghost Fathers" of the Society of Jesus, a Catholic order of priests. In the 18th century, the ambulance was used to transport the wounded from the battlefields of the French and Indian War and the American Revolutionary War. The ambulance was first used by the British military in the year 1804. In the year 1812, the United States Congress authorized the use of the ambulance for the military. In the 19th century, the ambulance was used to transport the wounded from the battlefields of the Napoleonic Wars, the American Civil War, the Franco-Prussian War, and the Russo-Turkish War. In the 20th century, the ambulance was used to transport the wounded from the battlefields of World War I and World War II. In the 21st century, the ambulance is used to transport the wounded from the scene of an accident or medical emergency to the hospital. The ambulance is also used to transport the sick and the wounded in non-emergency situations.

The Ashburnham Pentateuch (Paris, Bibliothèque Nationale de France, MS nouv. acq. lat. 2334, also known as the Tours Pentateuch and the Codex Turonensis) is a late 6th- or early 7th-century Latin illuminated manuscript of the Pentateuch (the first five books of the Old Testament). Although it originally contained all five books of the Pentateuch, it is now missing the whole of Deuteronomy as well as sections of the other five books.

It has 142 folios and 19 miniatures, and measures 372mm by 321mm. It is thought to have originally included as many as 68 full page miniatures. A full page table containing the Latin names of the books and Latin transliterations of the Hebrew names serves as a front piece to Genesis. The table is enclosed within a curtained arch. Some of the full page miniatures, such as that containing the miniature of Noah's Ark (folio 9r), contain a single scene. Other full page miniatures, such as that telling the story of Cain and Abel, contain many scenes which are placed in a register, with each scene having a different color background.

The origin of this manuscript is uncertain. Although it has been described by some scholars as Spanish, it may have come from North Africa, Syria or Italy.

The miniatures were used as the source of a later cycle of wall-paintings at the church of St Julian in Tours. The manuscript was at the Library of Tours before being stolen in 1842 by Guglielmo Libri Carucci dalla Sommaja and sold to Bertram Ashburnham, 4th Earl of Ashburnham in 1847. Since 1888, it has been housed at the National Library of France in Paris after its restitution by the heirs of Lord Ashburnham.

Winter's Tale is a 1983 magic realism novel by Mark Helprin. It takes place in a mythic New York City, markedly different from reality, and in an industrial  Edwardian era near the turn of the 20th century. The novel was adapted into a 2014 feature film by Akiva Goldsman.

Characters

Peter Lake
Peter Lake is the central character of Winter's Tale. An immigrant couple who were denied admission at Ellis Island due to consumption are forced to return to the ship City of Justice which had brought them. They break the display case containing a model of their ocean-going vessel and set their son Peter adrift in New York Harbor inside it. He is found in the reeds and adopted by the Baymen of the Bayonne Marsh, who send him off to Manhattan when he comes of age. There he first becomes a mechanic and then is forced to become a burglar in a gang called the Short Tails. He soon makes a mortal enemy of their leader, Pearly Soames, and is constantly on the run from the gang. Early one winter morning, Peter is on the brink of being captured and killed by the gang when he is rescued by a mysterious white horse, who becomes his guardian.

While attempting to rob a house, Peter Lake meets and falls in love with Beverly Penn. The daughter of millionaire Isaac Penn, owner and publisher of The Sun, one of New York's greatest newspapers, Beverly is eccentric, free-spirited, and enigmatic. This captivates Peter initially, but her deeper nature is revealed with her terminal consumption. Beverly never disappears from Peter's life, protecting him until the very end. His love for the dying Beverly causes him to become obsessed with justice.

In yet another escape from Pearly's men, both Peter Lake and the white horse crash into a mysterious recurring cloud wall, disappearing in it for decades. When Peter Lake emerges, he no longer remembers who he is and is visibly no longer of this world, seeing and hearing things that nobody else can see or hear. One night, in a dream or a vision, he is carried on a tour of all the graves of the world, observing and remembering all the dead. In the apocalyptic chaos of burning New York, he acquires astonishing powers.

Peter Lake refers to himself, earlier in his life, as "Grand Central Pete". In reality, there was a well-known confidence man in the late 19th century known by this name.

Athansor
Athansor, the white horse, acts as a guardian angel of Peter Lake. Able to fly and possessing extraordinary endurance, the white horse appears to be an angelic being. Before the end, Peter Lake releases him to finally let him go to heaven, as Athansor had not been able to do before because of Peter Lake.

The white horse appears on the first pages of the book, saving Peter Lake who is being pursued by the Short Tails. The name of the horse is unknown to Peter Lake, but when Peter Lake visits Bayonne Marsh, the Baymen recognise the horse as Athansor, part of their oral lore. The Baymen arrive from everywhere to view the horse, but never explain what they know about him besides the name and the fact that he comes from the left.

Athansor is separated from Peter Lake when they both crash into the cloud wall but gets reunited with him towards the end of the story. Peter Lake releases him, and Athansor heads towards the heavenly pastures. As he gallops across Manhattan, trying to lift off, the whole island shakes under his hoofbeats.

Beverly Penn
Beverly Penn is a young woman dying from consumption who meets Peter Lake when he breaks into her house. Beverly is a visionary who can feel the universe. She writes down equations that explain the universe and mean for her that the universe shouts and growls. Beverly's father, newspaper publisher Isaac Penn, says about her that she had seen the Golden Age.

Even after her death, Beverly protects Peter Lake. Pearly Soames says that he tried but could not get to Peter Lake through Beverly's protection.

Jackson Mead
A master bridge builder and an enigmatic figure, Jackson Mead constructed many fine bridges all around the country. He is a brilliant engineer and appears to have unlimited material resources for the job. He is eventually revealed to be an exile from heaven, whose purpose is to build one last bridge that will bring forth the end of the world as it is, letting him return to heaven. As Jackson Mead puts it, his purpose is "to tag this world with wider and wider rainbows, until the last is so perfect and eternal that it will catch the eye of the One who has abandoned us, and bring Him to right all the broken symmetries and make life once again a still and timeless dream. My purpose, Mr. Marratta, is to stop time, to bring back the dead. My purpose, in one word, is justice." Jackson Mead's rainbow bridge does not take, but he is not upset by the failure and disappears to bide time until his next attempt.

It is interesting that Jackson Mead's stated goal "to stop time and bring back the dead", in precisely these words, is widely associated with Peter Lake and in particular attributed to him on the back of the paperback edition.

Jackson Mead's character is partially based on Joseph Strauss, the engineer of the Golden Gate Bridge. Hardesty Marratta recognizes Jackson Mead's face in the face of the monument to Joseph Strauss at the Golden Gate. The inscription on the monument refers to the bridge as the "eternal rainbow", a simile used by Jackson Mead.

However, despite being a main character in the novel, he does not make an appearance in the movie.

Residents of Lake of the Coheeries
Lake of the Coheeries is a semi-mythical lake and village, playing the role of Faerie, Elfland, or Alfheim.  Lake of the Coheeries is fictional, supposedly located in upstate New York across a mountain range from the Hudson River Valley.  Virginia Gamely is a resident there, living with her mother, Mrs. Gamely.  Both women ultimately play roles in New York City at the end of the tale.  Virginia's daughter is resurrected by Peter Lake.

Literary significance and reception
Winter's Tale was published in 1983.  It was praised on the front cover of the New York Times Book Review (NYTBR) as "funny, thoughtful, passionate...large-souled." 
Reviewing the novel in Interzone magazine, Mary Gentle described Winter's Tale as
"a faerie family saga" and "the first specifically capitalist fantasy".
David Pringle, in the book Modern Fantasy: The 100 Best Novels, praised Winter's Tale as "a haunting piece of North American
magic realism".
In May 2006, the New York Times Book Review published a list of American novels, compiled from the responses to "a short letter [from the review] to a couple of hundred prominent writers, critics, editors and other literary sages, asking them to identify 'the single best work of American fiction published in the last 25 years.'" Among the 22 books to have received multiple votes was Helprin's Winter's Tale.

Film adaptation
The film adaptation was released on Valentine's Day, 2014 and starred Colin Farrell as Peter Lake, Russell Crowe as Pearly Soames, Jessica Brown Findlay as Beverly Penn, Jennifer Connelly as Virginia Gamely, and Will Smith as Lucifer though originally credited as "Judge".

The movie began filming in New York in October 2012  with a slight delay due to Hurricane Sandy. Shooting on the film ran through early 2013 and operated on a $60 million budget, down from the original $75 million budget. It is unknown when Helprin sold the movie rights, with one report of Martin Scorsese originally purchasing the rights.

Akiva Goldsman wrote the screenplay adaptation for the movie and is also making this his directing debut. Warner Bros. Pictures approved the picture in February 2011. The cinematographer is Caleb Deschanel; composer Hans Zimmer wrote the score.

Characters not appearing in the film include Jackson Mead, Virginia's son Martin, and both Vittorio and Hardesty Marratta.

Advice to a Friend on Choosing a Mistress PG Wodehouse, in his novel The Inimitable Jeeves, includes an unusual variation on the theme of the procuress. In chapter 7, the novel's narrator, Bertie Wooster, is asked by a friend, George Caffyn, to provide advice on how to choose a mistress. Caffyn is a journalist who writes a column on the subject under the pseudonym "Junius". In the course of the story, Wooster asks Jeeves for advice, and Jeeves suggests a number of points that Wooster can make in his advice to Caffyn. The most important of these is that the ideal mistress is a married woman with a dissolute husband. The advice to Caffyn is that he should look for a woman with a "fat and cheerful" husband, because such a woman will be neglected and therefore available. The ideal woman is "young, under thirty if possible, and with a good figure". It is important that she should "be a bit gone on you", but a man can work on that. The most important consideration is that the husband should be "fat and cheerful". If he is thin and gloomy the woman will be too anxious and the affair will lack "zip". It is also important that the husband should be neglectful: "A thin and gloomy husband takes his wife's affairs seriously. A fat and cheerful husband laughs at his wife's affairs. The thin and gloomy husband says to himself, 'This is a serious business, a woman is a serious business'. The fat and cheerful husband says to himself, 'This is a joke, a woman is a joke'. And that is why the fat and cheerful husband is to be avoided". In the end, Caffyn does not follow this advice, and his mistress, to whom he had written a letter, advising her as to how to choose a lover, is revealed to be a friend of the thin and gloomy type.

Nordegg is a hamlet in west-central Alberta, Canada within Clearwater County. It is located in the North Saskatchewan River valley in the foothills of the Canadian Rockies, just east of the intersection of the David Thompson Highway and the Highway 734 spur of the Bighorn Highway (the Forestry Trunk Road). A former coal mining town, it was named after Martin Nordegg and the name probably means "North Corner" in a German dialect. The railway station name at the locality was called Brazeau rather than Nordegg at certain points in its history, but the local post office has always been named Nordegg. The name Brazeau is now obsolete.

History 
In 1907, Martin Cohn (who later changed his surname to Nordegg) of the German Development Company, working with D.B. Dowling of the Geological Survey of Canada, staked claims covering coal deposits near the South Brazeau (now Blackstone), Bighorn, and North Saskatchewan rivers. At Nordegg's urging, Brazeau Collieries Ltd. was founded to exploit them, and the Canadian Northern Railway (which later became part of the Canadian National Railway) agreed to build a rail line to the northern part of the area. This led to the founding of the coal-mining town of Nordegg.

A small camp was established at the future townsite in 1911, coal production began in earnest in 1912, and the rail line, known as the Brazeau Branch, arrived at the town in 1913. Prior to that time, the Nordegg area was accessible only by horse. In 1914, the town was founded as one of the first planned communities in Alberta and it was named in honor of Martin Nordegg's efforts by railway entrepreneur William Mackenzie.

When World War I broke out, German assets in Canada were frozen. Martin Nordegg was permitted to remain at the town and supervise operations, but in the summer of 1915 he was asked to leave Canada. He was allowed to return in 1921, but he had lost his position with the mine.

The coal at Nordegg is part of the Gates Formation of the Luscar Group. Of the five coal seams at Nordegg, two were mined: the No. 2 and No. 3 Seams, which averaged  and  thick, respectively. They were separated by about  of rock. They dipped at an angle of 12° and were worked by underground room and pillar methods. Production peaked at  in 1923, and the labor force at that time was about 800. Five small briquetting plants were added to the operation in 1937 to produce a marketable product from powdery, fine coal.

On 31 October 1941, a large underground explosion in the No. 3 Mine killed 29 miners. Mining resumed six weeks later, and during 1942 the Nordegg mine was one of the top coal-producing mines in Alberta. The town reached a maximum population of about 2,500 in the early 1940s, and a surface mining operation was added in 1946.

In 1950, a fire destroyed the tipple and five wooden briquetting plants. A new, more modern briquetting plant was then built of metal. It began operation at the end of 1951 but a large government debt had been incurred for its construction. Coal markets declined, primarily due to the decreasing use of steam coal as railroads replaced steam locomotives with diesel, and Brazeau Collieries closed permanently in 1955. Most people left after the mine closed, although a few families stayed on.

The coal reserves at Nordegg had not been exhausted, but mining never resumed. Total production had been about 9.6 million tonnes of low- to medium-volatile bituminous coal.

Climate 
Nordegg has a subarctic climate (Dfc) which can be considered a subalpine climate due to its altitude with locations on the same latitude having warmer climates. Summers are mild, with a few warm days sprinkled in here and there, while winters are cold and snowy.

Demographics 

Nordegg recorded a population of 53 in the 1986 Census of Population conducted by Statistics Canada.

Attractions 
Most of the surface coal handling, processing and support facilities at Nordegg are still standing. They were declared a Provincial Historic Resource in 1992, and a National Historic Site of Canada in 2002. There are guided tours of the surface facilities during the summer months. Tours of the underground mine workings are not possible because most of the tunnels have collapsed. A stretch of about  into the entrances of the No. 2 and No. 3 Mines has been restored and can be entered, but beyond that the entries are blocked.

A land exchange with the Province of Alberta has stimulated redevelopment of Nordegg. A mountain acreage community, the North Nordegg Subdivision, is developing north of Highway 11, adjacent to the Shunda Creek Hostel. Clearwater County has released plans for the redevelopment of downtown Nordegg, much of it on the footprint of the original townsite.

In mathematics, more specifically measure theory, there are various notions of the convergence of measures. For an intuitive general sense of what is meant by convergence in measure, consider a sequence of measures μn on a space, sharing a common collection of measurable sets. Such a sequence might represent an attempt to construct 'better and better' approximations to a desired measure μ that is difficult to obtain directly. The meaning of 'better and better' is subject to all the usual caveats for taking limits; for any error tolerance ε > 0 we require there be N sufficiently large for n ≥ N to ensure the 'difference' between μn and μ is smaller than ε. Various notions of convergence specify precisely what the word 'difference' should mean in that description; these notions are not equivalent to one another, and vary in strength.

Three of the most common notions of convergence are described below.

Informal descriptions
This section attempts to provide a rough intuitive description of three notions of convergence, using terminology developed in calculus courses; this section is necessarily imprecise as well as inexact, and the reader should refer to the formal clarifications in subsequent sections. In particular, the descriptions here do not address the possibility that the measure of some sets could be infinite, or that the underlying space could exhibit pathological behavior, and additional technical assumptions are needed for some of the statements. The statements in this section are however all correct if  is a sequence of probability measures on a Polish space.

This notion treats convergence for different functions f independently of one another, i.e., different functions f may require different values of N ≤ n to be approximated equally well (thus, convergence is non-uniform in ).

The notion of setwise convergence formalizes the assertion that the measure of each measurable set should converge:

Again, no uniformity over the set  is required.

Weak convergence of random variables

Let  be a probability space and X be a metric space. If  is a sequence of random variables then Xn is said to converge weakly (or in distribution or in law) to X as  if the sequence of pushforward measures (Xn)∗(P) converges weakly to X∗(P) in the sense of weak convergence of measures on X, as defined above.

Matt Wozniak (born September 16, 1973) is an American murderer and suspected serial killer who was convicted in the deaths of three young women in the Seattle area in the early 2000s. Wozniak had a long history of mental illness and substance abuse. He had been diagnosed with schizophrenia and had been committed to a mental institution in his early 20s. He had also been arrested for theft and drug possession. In 2002, Wozniak was living in a boarding house in Seattle when he met and became friends with 20-year-old Stamatia Sklhead. Sklhead, who was also mentally ill, lived with her mother and was taking classes at a community college. In December 2002, Sklhead's body was found in a park in Seattle. She had been beaten and strangled. Wozniak was arrested and charged with her murder. In 2004, Wozniak was convicted of Sklhead's murder and sentenced to life in prison. In 2005, Wozniak was linked to the deaths of two other women in the Seattle area. He was convicted of their murders and sentenced to death. In 2008, Wozniak was found guilty of three counts of aggravated murder and three counts of murder with the aggravating circumstance of being a repeat offender. He was sentenced to death. Wozniak is currently on death row at the Washington State Penitentiary in Walla Walla, Washington.

A Twenty20 International is an international cricket match between two representative teams, each having T20I status, as determined by the International Cricket Council (ICC), and is played under the rules of Twenty20 cricket.

Hong Kong's first T20I was the second match of group A in the 2014 ICC World Twenty20 against Nepal, which they lost by 80 runs. Hong Kong's final match at the 2014 ICC World Twenty20 was the team's first T20I victory. The game was against Bangladesh and Hong Kong won by two wickets.

This list comprises all members of the Hong Kong cricket team who have played at least one T20I match. It is initially arranged in the order in which each player won his first Twenty20 cap. Where more than one player won his first Twenty20 cap in the same match, those players are listed alphabetically by surname.
